EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Individual severe thunderstorms that developed over South Central Wisconsin, producing hail up to 1 inch in diameter, merged into several squall lines that produced damaging wind gusts as they moved east through Southeast Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening hours of May 30th. Overall forcing for ascent over the region was provided by  differential vorticity advection ahead of a progressive 500 mb negatively-tilted trough, with the storms developing on the nose of a moderate to strong low-level jet ahead of the wave. Moderate instability, reflected in mixed-layer CAPE values of 1000 to 1200 J/kg, and favorable wind shear contributed to conditions that produced broken bands of thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts and downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported a few trees and power lines blown down by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 65 mph (56 knots) 5 miles west of Orfordville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed over north central Iowa along a cold front during the early morning hours of April 29th. As these storms moved east with the cold front, they produced large hail over the very southwest portion of Wisconsin. The largest hail reported was quarter sized in Hazel Green (Grant County).

Severe thunderstorms rolled across southwest Wisconsin during the afternoon of June 12th. These storms developed ahead of an area of low pressure that was moving east across the Upper Midwest. The storms produced a 60 mph wind gust in Port Andrew (Richland County) and some street flooding in Boscobel (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain caused some street flooding in Boscobel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the period of June 20th to June 28th, portions of southern Wisconsin experienced repeated rounds of thunderstorms, with rainfall of 1 inch to as much as 6 inches per round. The run-off from these heavy rain events caused area rivers and streams to flood. Flooding levels ranged from minor to moderate, with the Yahara River at Fulton in far northern Rock County going into major flood for a time. River stages climbed to between 2 feet to nearly 5 feet over flood stage. WHile most of the rivers fell back below flood stage by the end of the month, some rivers remained in flood into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gauge on the Pecatonica River near Martintown remained above flood stage from the morning of June 25th into early July. The river experienced minor flooding, cresting at 15.56 feet at 10:15 AM CDT on June 28th, which is 2.06 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of thunderstorms with heavy rain moved northwest to southeast from northwest Iowa County to southwest Green County during the early morning hours of June 22nd. This initial band weakened, but was followed by a second line that moved through  Grant County, but dropped more rain on portions of northwest Iowa, County and then through portions of  Lafayette and Green counties through mid-morning. The heavy rains closed many roads due to flooding, washing out a few, as well as causing mudslides. A severe wind gust was reported in Lafayette County near the end of the event due to a combination of the gradient winds of a wake low that developed behind the line interacting with the comma head vorticity maximum that developed at the north end of the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials called in with many reports of flooded roads due to the heavy rainfall. Roads on the west side of New Glarus were washed out.

A line of thunderstorms crossed southern Wisconsin during the morning hours of June 21st. A segment of the line briefly bowed out over portions of Sauk, Iowa and Lafayette counties, causing wind damage to trees and power lines in northwest Iowa County near Avoca. Vigorous synoptic forcing, especially strong, sloped frontogenesis from the surface up to 700 mb, accompanied a short wave trough through the region that drove a complex of strong to severe thunderstorms across Minnesota into southern Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Iowa County Emergency Manager reported numerous trees and power lines blown down by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 64 mph (56 knots).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Discrete supercell thunderstorms congealed into a line of severe thunderstorms over southwest Wisconsin that produced damaging wind gusts and large hail. The line of storms tracked through southern Wisconsin along and just north of a nearly-stationary warm front near the Wisconsin/Illinois border. Strong forcing for ascent was provided by the differential cyclonic vorticity advection associated with a compact mid/upper level trough. Bulk shear in the 0-6 km layer was on the order of 50-60 knots. In addition to the damaging winds and large hail, precipitable water values approached 2 inches, giving the storms sufficient moisture to produce heavy rainfall on the order of 1 to 2 inches in under one hour that caused localized street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jefferson County Emergency Manager reported many trees and power lines were blown down in Waterloo from damaging thunderstorm wind gusts that were estimated up to 65 mph ( knots).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of thunderstorms with heavy rain moved northwest to southeast from northwest Iowa County to southwest Green County during the early morning hours of June 22nd. This initial band weakened, but was followed by a second line that moved through  Grant County, but dropped more rain on portions of northwest Iowa, County and then through portions of  Lafayette and Green counties through mid-morning. The heavy rains closed many roads due to flooding, washing out a few, as well as causing mudslides. A severe wind gust was reported in Lafayette County near the end of the event due to a combination of the gradient winds of a wake low that developed behind the line interacting with the comma head vorticity maximum that developed at the north end of the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported many flooded roads due to the heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wake low developed behind an area of rain showers that moved through the region in the early morning hours of April 11th. There were reports of downed tree branches and scattered power outages due to downed power lines, as well as entire trees being downed by the powerful wind gusts that reached as high as 52 mph (45 knots) at Watertown Airport.

A large, but quickly moving area of precipitation in the form of freezing rain, sleet and snow, quickly moved from southeastern Minnesota, northeast across west central Wisconsin by noon. Initially, a mixture of sleet and freezing rain occurred, but some of the precipitation turned over to light snow and sleet as it moved northward into |west central Wisconsin.  There was a period in the afternoon where snowfall rates were one inch per hour, but most of the heavier snowfall ended by early Sunday evening. Some lingering light snow and freezing drizzle occurred in portions of west central Wisconsin through midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observers reported two to four inches of snow by the time the storm tapered off Sunday evening, but a mixture of sleet and freezing rain did occur during the onset Sunday afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of relatively mild and moist air aloft brought mainly freezing rain, with some light snow and sleet to southern Wisconsin. A glaze of ice from 0.05-0.15 inches accumulated on roadways and sidewalks. Numerous slide-offs and accidents occurred. Some schools were cancelled over southeast Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of 0.05-0.15 inches from freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow, along with a period of freezing rain, caused hazardous driving conditions across much of north central and northeast Wisconsin.  The heaviest snow fell in far north central Wisconsin where 4 to 6 inches of snow was followed by freezing rain and sleet.  The highest snowfall totals in Vilas County included an official report of 6.9 inches at Minocqua, and reports from the general public of 6.8 inches near Lac du Flambeau, 6.5 inches near Boulder Junction, and 6.0 inches at Arbor Vitae.||The precipitation formed as an area of low pressure moved across Wisconsin.  The same system dropped more than 2 feet of snow on northeast Minnesota.||Slick roads from a quick blast of snow and ice resulted in a multitude of traffic accidents in central Wisconsin.  One person was killed (indirect) and at least 2 people were injured (indirect) in the crashes.  Three roads were closed in Wausau (Marathon Co.) due to accidents and hazardous driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and freezing rain caused hazardous conditions on roads in central Wisconsin.  There were multiple traffic accidents, resulting in 1 fatality (indirect) and at least 2 injuries (indirect).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved from east central Wisconsin to eastern Upper Michigan. South winds brought unseasonably unstable air into southeast Wisconsin ahead of an approaching cold front. Multiple lines of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across southeast Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line produced an estimated 100 mph downburst just northeast of Allenton. A 10 x 12 foot section of roof decking was torn off a home. A 20 x 20 foot section of shingles were removed from a home along with damage to the chimney. 18 healthy trees in a one mile stretch were blown down in the same direction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving area of low pressure dropped 5 to 8 inches of snow on east central Wisconsin as it moved across the Ohio Valley into southeast Lower Michigan.  The highest snowfall totals from the storm included 7.2 inches near Forestville (Door Co.), 7.0 inches near Fish Creek (Door Co.), 6.8 inches at Chilton (Calumet Co.), and 6.0 inches at Two Rivers (Manitowoc Co.).

A glaze of ice from 0.05-0.15 inches accumulated on roadways and sidewalks. Numerous slide-offs and accidents occurred. Some schools were cancelled over southeast Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of 0.05-0.15 inches from freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Upper Midwest had a long duration winter storm event that developed Monday morning, December 2nd, and eventually evolved into a large storm system that moved from the Plains, northeast across the Upper Midwest and western Great Lakes region by late Wednesday night. ||A weak disturbance produced locally two to five inches across Polk, Rusk and Barron Counties of west central Wisconsin from Monday morning to Tuesday morning. A few locations in Barron County near Rice Lake and Cumberland received locally six inches from Monday's storm. ||By Tuesday, much warmer air from the southern United States produced precipitation in the form of sleet, rain and snow that fell through part of Wednesday afternoon as the surface low moved across west central Wisconsin. The mixture of precipitation eventually changed over to all snow Wednesday evening. However, the mixture of sleet and snow added up to locally 4 to 7 inches across portions of west central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals by Wednesday evening ranged from 4 to 7 inches snow and sleet across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved out of the Southern Plains into the Ohio River Valley from December 21st through the 22nd. Heavy snow on the northwest side of this system fell over parts of southwest into central Wisconsin. A band of 6 to 8 inches fell across Grant and Richland Counties into the southern sections of Adams and Juneau Counties. The highest reported total was 7.8 inches in Fennimore (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 6 to 7 inches of snow fell across Richland County. The highest reported total was 7 inches just northwest of Richland Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northerly flow over western Lake Superior brought heavy lake effect snow to parts of northwest Wisconsin near Lake Superior.  Snowfall amounts included 12.5 in Gile, WI (Iron County) and 6 to 7 in Mellen, WI (Ashland County).

Snowfall amounts averaged 14 to 16 inches with 1 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation across western Wisconsin in the counties still affected by the drought. With the frozen ground, only a small amount of this precipitation was absorbed by the soil. Soil conditions improved enough across southern Grant County for that area to be removed from the severe drought. No change in the drought conditions occurred elsewhere and a severe to extreme drought remained across Buffalo, Taylor and Trempealeau Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across Buffalo County averaged 14 to 16 inches with 1.25 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation during February. With the frozen ground, little to none of this moisture was absorbed by the soil. No changed in the drought conditions occurred with the entire county remaining in a severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed over north central Iowa along a cold front during the early morning hours of April 29th. As these storms moved east with the cold front, they produced large hail over the very southwest portion of Wisconsin. The largest hail reported was quarter sized in Hazel Green (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter sized hail fell for a couple of minutes in Hazel Green.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of May 31st. These storms developed in a complex surface pattern across the Upper Midwest as a pre-frontal surface trough, dry line and cold front all passed through the area. The storms primarily produced large hail, although a funnel cloud was sighted near Merrillan (Jackson County). The largest hail reported was half dollar sized near Independence (Trempealeau County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted northwest of Merrillan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of May 31st. These storms developed in a complex surface pattern across the Upper Midwest as a pre-frontal surface trough, dry line and cold front all passed through the area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell north of Fountain City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Individual severe thunderstorms that developed over South Central Wisconsin, producing hail up to 1 inch in diameter, merged into several squall lines that produced damaging wind gusts as they moved east through Southeast Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening hours of May 30th. Overall forcing for ascent over the region was provided by  differential vorticity advection ahead of a progressive 500 mb negatively-tilted trough, with the storms developing on the nose of a moderate to strong low-level jet ahead of the wave. Moderate instability, reflected in mixed-layer CAPE values of 1000 to 1200 J/kg, and favorable wind shear contributed to conditions that produced broken bands of thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts and downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A newspaper article reported 23 trees and numerous power-lines blown down by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 65 mph (56 knots) about 1 mile west-northwest of Dane County Regional Airport. Some cars and homes were also damaged by the downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance triggered thunderstorms that moved across central and north central Wisconsin.  The storms produced isolated severe weather across north central Wisconsin, including large hail, wind damage and heavy rainfall.  The heavy rain in Merrill (Lincoln Co.) caused some street flooding.  Lightning in advance of one of the storms stuck a boy in rural Marathon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked trees onto power lines and a house.  The time of this event was estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance passed across the area during peak daytime heating.  The unstable air, the upper disturbance, and a dew point boundary contributed to the development of thunderstorms that produced large hail and a funnel cloud in east central Wisconsin.  Quite a bit of quarter to walnut size hail fell in and near Appleton, and there was even some golf ball to nearly tennis ball size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced quarter size hail as they moved through the Kimberly area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance passed across the area during peak daytime heating.  The unstable air, the upper disturbance, and a dew point boundary contributed to the development of thunderstorms that produced large hail and a funnel cloud in east central Wisconsin.  Quite a bit of quarter to walnut size hail fell in and near Appleton, and there was even some golf ball to nearly tennis ball size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced hail up to quarter size between Royalton and Northport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced penny size hail at College Avenue and Lynndale Drive in Appleton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms brought damaging winds across much of South Central Wisconsin during the afternoon of May 19th. The wind gusts, between 60 mph and 75 mph (52 knots to 65 knots), blew down numerous trees and power lines. The severe thunderstorms developed on the nose of a strengthening low-level jet ahead of the first of a series of short-wave troughs/impulses lifting through the western Great Lakes region on the eastern side of a deep upper-level trough over the Dakotas. The low-level winds brought in an unstable air mass, with surface-based CAPE of 1000 to 1300 J/kg, with 0-6km shear of 35 to 40 knots supporting sustained updrafts and rotating storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported a few trees, tree branches and power lines downed by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph (52 knots).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several lines of severe thunderstorms with damaging winds affected South Central Wisconsin during the late evening hours of May 19th, lasting into the early morning hours of May 20th. Some structural damage occurred, with many trees and power lines downed by winds that were sustained for 10 to 20 minutes at a time up to 44 mph (38 knots),  and gusting to 60 mph (52 knots). The lines of severe storms were moving around 40 knots (46 mph) driven a by a 50-60 knot low-level jet ahead of one of a series of a short-wave impulses that crossed the region. The combination of actual storm speed and the momentum transfer of the strong low-level jet winds being brought down to the surface with the storm precipitation produced the damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported trees downed by powerful thunderstorm winds that gusted to an estimated 65 mph (56 knots) along a path from Reedsburg to 3 miles wesdt of Lake Delton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved into Iowa and Sauk counties during the early evening hours of May 29th, with damaging wind gusts estimated up to 80 mph (70 knots) knocking down trees and power lines as well as causing roof damage to several homes and structural damage to outbuildings on a few farms. The line of severe storms had developed to the west of Wisconsin during the day, ahead of a mesoscale convective vorticity maximum. The increased southerly low-level flow ahead of this wave drew warm, moist and unstable air into the region that converged over western Wisconsin, with lift aided by the upper divergence associated with the exit region of an upper-level jet. The line weakened as it moved east into cooler and more stable air over eastern portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sauk County 911 center received a report of damage to a building in Plain due to thunderstorm wind gusts that were estimated at 65 mph (56 knots).

The line of thunderstorms produced a gustnado 7 miles east of Baraboo, however only some minor tree damage was reported with the gustnado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Individual severe thunderstorms that developed over South Central Wisconsin, producing hail up to 1 inch in diameter, merged into several squall lines that produced damaging wind gusts as they moved east through Southeast Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening hours of May 30th. Overall forcing for ascent over the region was provided by  differential vorticity advection ahead of a progressive 500 mb negatively-tilted trough, with the storms developing on the nose of a moderate to strong low-level jet ahead of the wave. Moderate instability, reflected in mixed-layer CAPE values of 1000 to 1200 J/kg, and favorable wind shear contributed to conditions that produced broken bands of thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts and downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported several large tree branches in excess of 6 inches in diameter downed by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph (52 knots) 7 miles southwest of the State Capitol in Madison.  A couple power-lines were snapped by the tree debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually late and strong winter storm brought a narrow band of heavy snow of up to about 20 inches to parts of northwest Wisconsin.  The main band of over 14 inches of snow stretched from about Rochester, MN to Ashland, WI.  Storm reports included 18 inches near Clam Lake, 18 inches near Mellen, 17.4 inches near Ashland, 17.3 inches near Saxon, 15.4 inches near Upson, 14.2 inches near Hayward, 13 inches near Odanah, and 10.3 inches near Bayfield.

A small tornado crossed the Mississippi River from Iowa hitting a campground just north of Bagley (Grant County). This tornado was part of a larger severe weather outbreak that occurred across much of western Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of May 29th. These storms developed in a warm and moist airmass behind a warm front that was moving north across the Upper Midwest. In addition to the tornado, a pole barn and other structures were damaged near Whitehall (Trempealeau County) and structural damage to buildings also occurred near Richland Center (Richland County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Just south of Whitehall, a pole barn was blown down, other large structures were damaged and numerous tree limbs were blown onto the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved into western Wisconsin during the afternoon and increased in intensity and coverage across central and northern Wisconsin as they encountered a more unstable air mass.  The storms produced heavy rainfall and hail to nickel size as they moved eastward across the state.  Street flooding occurred in Neenah (Winnebago Co.), Clintonville (Waupaca Co.), and Stevens Point (Portage Co.) as a result of the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell south of Clintonville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Trempealeau River out of its banks in Dodge. The river crested almost a foot over flood stage at 9.96 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along a weak boundary and produced several funnel clouds and four weak tornadoes. Damage by the tornadoes was minimal, affecting mainly wooded areas and open fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just east of Highway 41 near Swede Town Road and County Road A in Oconto County and moved along an eastward path toward the bay. The tornado was observed by an eyewitness. Dozens of trees were snapped and uprooted along the path of the tornado (DI 27, DOD 3-4 and DI 28, DOD 3-4). The tornado also blew over a recreational vehicle trailer. Average path width of the tornado was 75 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of May 31st. These storms developed in a complex surface pattern across the Upper Midwest as a pre-frontal surface trough, dry line and cold front all passed through the area. The storms primarily produced large hail, although a funnel cloud was sighted near Merrillan (Jackson County). The largest hail reported was half dollar sized near Independence (Trempealeau County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along a weak boundary and produced several funnel clouds and four weak tornadoes. Damage by the tornadoes was minimal, affecting mainly wooded areas and open fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado briefly touched down in an open field just west of Interstate 39 in far southern Portage County. The tornado, which was observed by a storm spotter, did no damage. The average path width of the tornado was estimated to be 10 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of May 31st. These storms developed in a complex surface pattern across the Upper Midwest as a pre-frontal surface trough, dry line and cold front all passed through the area. The storms primarily produced large hail, although a funnel cloud was sighted near Merrillan (Jackson County). The largest hail reported was half dollar sized near Independence (Trempealeau County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell in Independence.

Several lines of severe thunderstorms with damaging winds affected South Central Wisconsin during the late evening hours of May 19th, lasting into the early morning hours of May 20th. Some structural damage occurred, with many trees and power lines downed by winds that were sustained for 10 to 20 minutes at a time up to 44 mph (38 knots),  and gusting to 60 mph (52 knots). The lines of severe storms were moving around 40 knots (46 mph) driven a by a 50-60 knot low-level jet ahead of one of a series of a short-wave impulses that crossed the region. The combination of actual storm speed and the momentum transfer of the strong low-level jet winds being brought down to the surface with the storm precipitation produced the damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported trees downed by powerful thunderstorm winds that gusted to an estimated 65 mph (56 knots) about 3 miles south-southwest of Lone Rock. One of the downed trees blocked State Highway 130 for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance passed across the area during peak daytime heating.  The unstable air, the upper disturbance, and a dew point boundary contributed to the development of thunderstorms that produced large hail and a funnel cloud in east central Wisconsin.  Quite a bit of quarter to walnut size hail fell in and near Appleton, and there was even some golf ball to nearly tennis ball size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced half dollar size hail as it passed southwest of Mattoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several lines of severe thunderstorms crossed southern Wisconsin during the evening hours of May 14th. The storms produced damaging wind gusts up to 70 to 75 mph (60 to 65 knots). Widespread damage was reported in swaths up to 6 miles in width, which included structural damage to homes and farm buildings as well as downed trees and power lines due to the powerful winds. WE Energies reported 23,000 customers in Dodge, Jefferson, Waukesha and Milwaukee counties were without power at the height of the storm. The severe thunderstorms formed along a warm front that extended from weak low pressure in northwest Wisconsin south of Superior to near Chicago. While wind shear and instability were marginal, evaporative cooling as the rain fell into dry air in the lower levels produced prime conditions for downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Firefighters reported numerous trees, power lines and some power poles downed by thunderstorm wind gusts, estimated at 75 mph (65 knots), along a 5-mile wide path from 2 miles northwest of Roxbury to 2.5 miles east of Marshall. An old barn was completely blown down; a shed was moved 4 feet off of it's foundation; and roof shingles, siding and downspouts were torn off of a couple of homes in the village of Dane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Individual severe thunderstorms that developed over South Central Wisconsin, producing hail up to 1 inch in diameter, merged into several squall lines that produced damaging wind gusts as they moved east through Southeast Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening hours of May 30th. Overall forcing for ascent over the region was provided by  differential vorticity advection ahead of a progressive 500 mb negatively-tilted trough, with the storms developing on the nose of a moderate to strong low-level jet ahead of the wave. Moderate instability, reflected in mixed-layer CAPE values of 1000 to 1200 J/kg, and favorable wind shear contributed to conditions that produced broken bands of thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts and downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported trees downed by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 65 mph (56 knots) in Randolph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in moist and unstable air ahead of an approaching trough and a strong cold front.  The storms produced a funnel cloud, isolated wind damage, and large hail as they moved across northeast Wisconsin.  A funnel cloud was spotted west of Deerbrook (Langlade Co.), and thunderstorms winds downed trees near Keshena (Menominee Co.) and Oconto Falls (Oconto Co.).  Golf ball size hail fell near Summit Lake (Langlade Co.), and quarter size hail fell near Abrams (Oconto Co.) and Byrant (Langlade Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell about 10 miles north of Keshena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree next to a home in Spread Eagle during the morning, setting the house ablaze.  No one was in the house when the fire started, but the house was destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning stuck a gas line, causing a fire that destroyed the adjacent house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in a very moist and unstable air mass ahead of an approaching cold front and low pressure system.  The storms produced wind damage from a wet microburst, penny to nickel size hail, and heavy rainfall.  The microburst downed about 30 trees and dropped penny size hail in Forest County, between Crandon and Wabeno.  Numerous locations received more than 2 inches of rain in a 3 to 4 hour period, and the roof of a building in Appleton (Outagamie Co.) collapsed from the weight of the rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell at North Trout Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of snow, sleet and rain, including some thunderstorms, moved across southern Minnesota during the evening of Tuesday, April 9th. On the northern edge of the precipitation bands, a few inches of snow fell across the southern portion of the Twin Cities Metro area, and eastward into west central Wisconsin. This area moved quickly eastward across the western Great Lakes by early Wednesday morning, April 10th.||A break in the precipitation occurred Wednesday afternoon, through Wednesday evening, before another area of snow developed late Wednesday, and continued through Thursday morning.  Most of the heavy snowfall was in Minnesota, with mostly sleet and freezing rain across west central Wisconsin until the afternoon. The heavy sleet and freezing rain that occurred briefly Thursday morning across far east central Minnesota and west central Wisconsin kept snowfall amounts lower.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 3 to 5 inches across the county, along with some ice accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed during the early afternoon across southeast Minnesota and then intensified as they moved across portions of western Wisconsin. At their strongest, the storms produced large spiky hailstones with some estimated to have almost 3 inch diameters. Despite the large size, very little damage occurred except in Onalaska (La Crosse County) where two car dealerships were hit by the large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail that ranged in diameter from 1.5 to 2 inches fell at Valley View Mall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down west of Curtiss along County Road P.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped pea to quarter size hail.  Wind gusts knocked down a 6 inch diameter tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front during the afternoon of Monday, July 22nd across west central Wisconsin. Ample wind shear created the environment for supercell thunderstorms, including one brief touchdown west of Augusta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down near the intersection of County Road VV and Senske Road, approximately 4.3 miles west-southwest of Augusta. The tornado only lasted a minute causing damage to apple trees, large oak trees and a nearby corn field as it moved southeast for approximately 1/4 mile. A storm chaser took video of the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought large hail and damaging winds to parts of northwest Wisconsin on July 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts blew down numerous trees in Montreal and in the area Hundreds of homes and businesses lost power due to downed power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell in Appleton during the early morning of June 8th, mainly between 0315CST and 0700CST. The 24-hour rainfall total ending at 0600CST was 2.35 inches.  The weight of the rainwater caused the roof of a building to collapse around 0900CST.  The collapse caused a gas leak, and the Appleton building inspector declared 3 buildings uninhabitable while contractors examined the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot dome of high pressure that covered most of the eastern U.S. between July 16th and July 19th produced high temperatures in the upper 80s to lower 90s each day over all of southern Wisconsin, with widespread heat indices of 95 to 99. Locations in Milwaukee, Jefferson and Kenosha counties reached heat index values between 100 and 102. During this period, 5 deaths were being investigated as possibly being heat-related in Milwaukee County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms crossed southern Wisconsin during the afternoon hours of July 21st. The storms produced damaging wind gusts as well as hail up to 1 inch. Two people were hurt when their horses were struck by lightning while riding in the Kettle Moraine Forest near Eagle in Waukesha County. The thunderstorms formed in a region of focused warm air advection, with lift aided by vorticity advection from a short wave that tracked across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walworth County 911 Call Center received reports of trees and power lines downed by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph (52 knots) in East Troy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of severe thunderstorms produced a tornado in Columbia County, as well damaging winds over South Central Wisconsin during the evening hours of June 22nd. The complex developed on the nose of a 30 knot low-level jet ahead of a short wave trough impinging on a stationary boundary over northern Illinois that began to lift north as a warm front. Effective shear of 30 knots to sustain updrafts, with mixed layer CAPE values reaching 2000 to 3000 J/kg with steep mid-level lapse rates contributed to sufficient instability for severe thunderstorms. Precipitable water values between 1.5 inch to 2 inches also contributed to heavy rain that caused flash flooding in eastern Sauk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak rain-wrapped tornado spun up 2.17 miles north-northeast of Arlington northwest of the intersection of Krier Rd. and Thiele Ed and moved northeast for about 3.5 miles before dissipating midway between King Road and Mud Lake just southwest of Mud Lake.  The ending location would be 4 miles east of Poynette.  This tornado crossed STH 22 at the intersection of Loveland Road and then curved more north-northeast and tracked about 300 yards just east of the intersection of STH 22 and CTH CS.  Several outbuildings were damaged and a 3000 lb farm implement was tossed about 500 feet.  Due to wet soils, two power-poles were partially tipped over.  A couple vehicles were damaged and other farm equipment scattered around. One home sustained minor siding and roof shingle damage.  Otherwise, the usual tree damage occurred and a couple trees were uprooted.  The corn crop was only a few inches in height but some stalks were broken off at ground level.  Tree damage was consistent with DI 27 Hardwood Trees (TH) and DOD3 which suggests wind speeds of 90 to 95 mph.  The path length was about 3.7 miles and maximum width was 125 yards.  The average width was about 50 yards.

The thunderstorms formed on the nose of a strengthening low-level jet ahead of a negatively-tilted short wave trough. Severe storms were supported by large CAPE values of 2000-3000 J/kg and 0 km to 6 km shear around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at the Janesville Airport recorded a wind gust of 50 knots (58 mph).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person passed away from heat related causes near Osseo (Trempealeau County) during a heat wave in the middle of July. The victim apparently fell outside a house on a day when full sunshine was occurring. The heat index at the nearest automated weather observing equipment at the Black River Falls airport (Jackson County) approached 100 in the afternoon. Air temperatures routinely topped out between 90 and 95 degrees with overnight lows of 70 to 75.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person passed away from heat related causes near Osseo. The victim fell outside and was unable to get up on a day with full sunshine and when the heat index approached 100 at the automated weather observing equipment at the Black River Falls airport. Air temperatures topped out between 90 and 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable air mass combined with an upper level disturbance and a weak surface boundary to produce thunderstorms.  Some of the storms produced large, destructive hail and isolated wind damage.  Quarter to golf ball size hail damaged vehicles in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), but east central Wisconsin was hardest hit by the storms.  Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines in the Birnamwood (Shawano Co.) and Clintonville (Waupaca Co.) areas, and golf ball to tennis ball size hail caused considerable damage to vehicles, homes and businesses around the city of Manitowoc (Manitowoc Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell south of Kewaunee along the shore of Lake Michigan.

Most of the hail that fell was penny to nickel sized. Locally heavy rain caused street flooding to occur in the city of La Crosse and a minor mud slide south of Genoa (Vernon County) that covered part of State Highway 35.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance triggered thunderstorms that moved across central and north central Wisconsin.  The storms produced isolated severe weather across north central Wisconsin, including large hail, wind damage and heavy rainfall.  The heavy rain in Merrill (Lincoln Co.) caused some street flooding.  Lightning in advance of one of the storms stuck a boy in rural Marathon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in and around Merrill caused street flooding.  The AWOS at Merrill Municipal Airport recorded 1.77 inches of rain between 1555CST and 1655CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second night in a row, thunderstorms with very heavy rain rolled across southwest Wisconsin producing widespread flash flooding during the evening of June 22nd. At least three rounds of thunderstorms moved across the area dumping another 3 to 4 inches of rain on ground that was saturated from heavy rains that fell less than 24 hours before. Numerous mud slides occurred in Richland and Crawford Counties causing state and county highways to be closed. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency following the flooding in Crawford, Grant and Richland Counties. FEMA declared a federal disaster area for the following counties: Crawford, Grant, Richland and Vernon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at the Genoa Lock and Dam reported 3.2 inches of rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall in a short period of time caused a mudslide on Highway 53 just south of the Eau Claire/Chippewa county line. The southbound lanes were closed for about three hours as crews removed more than 30 truckloads of soil.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second night in a row, thunderstorms with very heavy rain rolled across southwest Wisconsin producing widespread flash flooding during the evening of June 22nd. At least three rounds of thunderstorms moved across the area dumping another 3 to 4 inches of rain on ground that was saturated from heavy rains that fell less than 24 hours before. Numerous mud slides occurred in Richland and Crawford Counties causing state and county highways to be closed. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency following the flooding in Crawford, Grant and Richland Counties. FEMA declared a federal disaster area for the following counties: Crawford, Grant, Richland and Vernon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Kickapoo River out of its banks in Gays Mills. The river crested around three and a half feet over flood stage at 16.63 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms pulsed up to severe levels during the early morning hours of June 25th. The storms produced damaging wind gusts in Rock County. The thunderstorms formed on the nose of a strengthening low-level jet ahead of a negatively-tilted short wave trough. Severe storms were supported by large CAPE values of 2000-3000 J/kg and 0 km to 6 km shear around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rock County Emergency manager reported many large trees uprooted and branches downed by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 65 mph (56 knots) along a path from Clinton to 4 miles south of Clinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced flooding rains over portions of South Central Wisconsin and far southern Southeast Wisconsin. The storms formed on moderate low-level flow ahead of short wave trough that interacted with an existing outflow boundary over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS and Truax Field in Madison recorded a thunderstorm wind gust at 59 mph (51 knots).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary band of thunderstorms developed across southwest Wisconsin during the evening of June 21st. These storms remained in place until another line of storms pushed in from the west during the early morning hours of the 22nd. Rainfall amounts exceeded six inches with flash flooding reported in parts of Crawford, Grant and Richland Counties. The area in and around Boscobel (Grant County) was hit extremely hard by the flooding. 650 homes were impacted by the flooding with two dozen families displaced, most homes had basements full of water with some having the water reach the first level. Roads in the region had numerous washouts and some bridges were blown out. Rescue crews had to save 54 canoeists who became stranded on the Wisconsin River due to rising waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across southwest Wisconsin during the evening of June 24th into the early morning hours of June 25th. The heavy rain produced yet more flash flooding that led to the evacuation of a trailer home near Bagley (Grant County).  Many roads became covered with water in Crawford and Grant Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced street flooding in Prairie du Chien that caused some vehicles to become stalled. The automated weather observing equipment at the Prairie du Chien airport measured 1.71 inches of rain in less than two hours. A mudslide in the southern part of the county closed U.S. Highway 61 between State Highway 60 and Boscobel in Grant County. The slide was estimated to be 200 yards long.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms rolled across southwest Wisconsin during the afternoon of June 12th. These storms developed ahead of an area of low pressure that was moving east across the Upper Midwest. The storms produced a 60 mph wind gust in Port Andrew (Richland County) and some street flooding in Boscobel (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 60 mph wind gust occurred in Port Andrew.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that developed over northwest Minnesota during the evening of June 20th, moved across western Wisconsin during the early morning hours of the 21st. These storms produced large hail and winds strong enough to cause damage. Trees were blown down in the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County) and a pontoon boat was flipped by the high winds on Lake Onalaska (La Crosse County). Most of the hail that fell was penny to nickel sized. Locally heavy rain caused street flooding to occur in the city of La Crosse and a minor mud slide south of Genoa (Vernon County) that covered part of State Highway 35.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of severe thunderstorms produced a tornado in Columbia County, as well damaging winds over South Central Wisconsin during the evening hours of June 22nd. The complex developed on the nose of a 30 knot low-level jet ahead of a short wave trough impinging on a stationary boundary over northern Illinois that began to lift north as a warm front. Effective shear of 30 knots to sustain updrafts, with mixed layer CAPE values reaching 2000 to 3000 J/kg with steep mid-level lapse rates contributed to sufficient instability for severe thunderstorms. Precipitable water values between 1.5 inch to 2 inches also contributed to heavy rain that caused flash flooding in eastern Sauk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported trees down in Mazomanie due to thunderstorm wind gusts estimated up to 60 mph (56 knots).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Discrete supercell thunderstorms congealed into a line of severe thunderstorms over southwest Wisconsin that produced damaging wind gusts and large hail. The line of storms tracked through southern Wisconsin along and just north of a nearly-stationary warm front near the Wisconsin/Illinois border. Strong forcing for ascent was provided by the differential cyclonic vorticity advection associated with a compact mid/upper level trough. Bulk shear in the 0-6 km layer was on the order of 50-60 knots. In addition to the damaging winds and large hail, precipitable water values approached 2 inches, giving the storms sufficient moisture to produce heavy rainfall on the order of 1 to 2 inches in under one hour that caused localized street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dodge County Emergency Manager reported many trees and power lines were blown down along a 10 mile-wide path from 5 miles southwest of Fox Lake to 4 miles south of Juneau from damaging thunderstorm wind gusts that were estimated up to 70 mph (61 knots).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved into South Central Wisconsin along a cold front during the evening hours of June 15th. Additional forcing for ascent that helped produce thunderstorms along the front was provided by the differential cyclonic vorticity ahead of a short wave trough and upper level divergence in the right entrance region of an upper level jet acting on moderately unstable air in place over the region, with CAPE values between 1000 and 1300 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a report from the public of large hail estimated to be 1 inch in diameter 2 miles west of Lake Mills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary band of thunderstorms developed across southwest Wisconsin during the evening of June 21st. These storms remained in place until another line of storms pushed in from the west during the early morning hours of the 22nd. Rainfall amounts exceeded six inches with flash flooding reported in parts of Crawford, Grant and Richland Counties. The area in and around Boscobel (Grant County) was hit extremely hard by the flooding. 650 homes were impacted by the flooding with two dozen families displaced, most homes had basements full of water with some having the water reach the first level. Roads in the region had numerous washouts and some bridges were blown out. Rescue crews had to save 54 canoeists who became stranded on the Wisconsin River due to rising waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain produced flash flooding across the northeast part of Grant County. |In Boscobel, numerous houses became surrounded by water with evacuations needed, most streets were flooded and cars were under water. 650 homes were impacted by the flood waters with two dozen families displaced. County Roads M and S near Boscobel were impassable due to high water and several other county roads between Blue River and Fennimore were covered with water and impassable. Portions of County Road C southwest of Woodman were under water. The flooding washed out sections of State Highway 133 outside of Woodman and Blue River. Debris was washed down Glass Hollow and Dry Hollow Creeks where it became lodged across railroad bridges in Bagley producing flooding in the town. The railroad tracks through Woodman were washed out. Flooding debris blocked some roads around Patch Grove. 54 canoeists had to be rescued from the Wisconsin River because of rising water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of thunderstorms with heavy rain moved northwest to southeast from northwest Iowa County to southwest Green County during the early morning hours of June 22nd. This initial band weakened, but was followed by a second line that moved through  Grant County, but dropped more rain on portions of northwest Iowa, County and then through portions of  Lafayette and Green counties through mid-morning. The heavy rains closed many roads due to flooding, washing out a few, as well as causing mudslides. A severe wind gust was reported in Lafayette County near the end of the event due to a combination of the gradient winds of a wake low that developed behind the line interacting with the comma head vorticity maximum that developed at the north end of the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported many flooded roads, with some washed outacross Iowa County. State Highways 130 and 133 were closed by flooding and mudslides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second night in a row, thunderstorms with very heavy rain rolled across southwest Wisconsin producing widespread flash flooding during the evening of June 22nd. At least three rounds of thunderstorms moved across the area dumping another 3 to 4 inches of rain on ground that was saturated from heavy rains that fell less than 24 hours before. Numerous mud slides occurred in Richland and Crawford Counties causing state and county highways to be closed. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency following the flooding in Crawford, Grant and Richland Counties. FEMA declared a federal disaster area for the following counties: Crawford, Grant, Richland and Vernon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain pushed small creeks out of their banks across southern Trempealeau County. Water over the road caused County Highway T to be closed in several locations north of Galesville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding across the southwest half of Richland County. Numerous streams were forced out of their banks with water reported over County Highway JJ between Gotham and Lone Rock, State Highway 56 near Gillingham and the intersection of County Highways O and OO near Richland Center. Mud slides closed County Highway O northwest of Gotham. State Highway 60 between Gotham and the Crawford County line southwest of Westport was closed by a mudslide with other damage reducing the road to one lane at times.

Quarter to golf ball size hail damaged vehicles in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), but east central Wisconsin was hardest hit by the storms.  Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines in the Birnamwood (Shawano Co.) and Clintonville (Waupaca Co.) areas, and golf ball to tennis ball size hail caused considerable damage to vehicles, homes and businesses around the city of Manitowoc (Manitowoc Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell along State Highway 17, near the Oneida County/Lincoln County border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air spread into southern Wisconsin behind deep low pressure that tracked to the north of the state.  About 2,000 feet above ground level, 925 mb temperatures fell to -17C to -22C���with 925 mb winds of 20 to 30 knots.  Momentum transfer of these winds to the surface produced gusty northwest winds, which combined with surface temperatures in the single digits below zero to produce wind chills between -20 to -30.  The frigid wind chills began the morning of January 21st across far northern Southeast Wisconsin and most of South-central Wisconsin���then spread across the remainder of southern Wisconsin during the evening of January 21st and continued everywhere into the morning hours of January 22nd.||An interesting side note to this cold outbreak was the fact that it was one of the relatively few times Milwaukee recorded a low temperature below zero without having a snow cover.

Strong, widespread west to northwest wind gusts in the 45 to 50 mph (39 to 43 knots) range occurred over Southern Wisconsin during the evening of January 19th.  These winds, behind a strong cold front, ushered in the coldest air to that point of the winter season.  Newspaper articles reported scattered power outages as wind-broken tree limbs impacted power-lines.  The strong winds were related to a tightening pressure gradient around a low pressure system that rapidly deepened as it tracked across the Upper Peninsula of Michigan to southwest Quebec.

Snow affecting Southern Wisconsin on January 27th became mixed with some freezing rain and sleet which resulted in hazardous driving conditions.  There were brief heavy showers of sleet and bursts of snow with isolated elevated thunderstorms.  The mix of snow...sleet and freezing rain left a glaze 0.1 to 0.2 inch thick on area roads and exposed surfaces...with snow accumulations of 1/2 inch to nearly 2 inches adding to the slick travel conditions.  The precipitation changed over to all rain from south to north during the evening and overnight hours as low-level warm air overspread the region.  The warm air was related to a warm front moving north through the western Great Lake Region.  In fact, about 5,000 feet above the ground there was a 50-knot (58 mph) low-level southerly jet that provide the energy and moisture for the isolated thunderstorms.

Despite above normal precipitation for March 2013, severe drought (D2) conditions that developed in parts of Lincoln County, Oneida County and Vilas County in November 2012 continued into April 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite another month of above normal precipitation across north central Wisconsin, southwest Vilas County remained in the severe drought (D2) category throughout March 2013.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed over to snow over most of Southeast Wisconsin and far southeast portions of South-central Wisconsin as low pressure followed a favored track from southern Missouri to eastern Lower Michigan during the day on January 30th.   Snow accumulations between 3 and 5 inches were reported along and southeast of a Rock County to Ozaukee County line.  A slower transition from rain to snow limited total snow accumulations to 1 to 3 inches in Milwaukee...Racine and Kenosha counties.  Increasing northwest winds behind the low pressure produced areas of blowing and drifting snow as wind gusts reached 30 to 35 mph (26 to 29 knots).  Roads glazed over as the standing water from early rains froze when temperatures dropped, which along with the blowing and drifting snow was sufficient, when combined with the accumulating snow, to create hazardous travel conditions.  Numerous accidents and spin-offs were reported by area law enforcement officials, including an injury accident in Jefferson County.  Meteorologically, deep forcing with sloped frontogenesis and strong layer Q-vector convergence, combined with some convective instability in and above the dendritic growth zone, produced moderate to briefly intense snowfall intensities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated 5.0 inches of total snow accumulation in an unincorporated area in the northwest corner of Washington County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 5 and 8 inches of snow fell across a small section of western Wisconsin on February 21st into the 22nd. The snow was produced by an area of low pressure and warm front that moved northeast across the Upper Midwest. The heaviest snow fell during the overnight hours of the 21st when snowfall rates of an inch per hour were common. The highest reported snowfall amount was 8.3 inches at Mississippi River Lock and Dam 4 near Alma (Buffalo County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer and cooperative observers reported between 5 and 8 inches of snow across the county. The highest reported total was 8.3 inches at Mississippi River Lock and Dam 4 near Alma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of relatively warm and moist air aloft led to the development of light to moderate snow during the morning rush hour with 1-3 inches accumulating. Roads became snow covered and numerous slide-offs and accidents occurred, especially in the Madison area and the Milwaukee Metropolitan area.  Two indirect deaths occurred with vehicle mishaps in Dane County. Since this was the first snow event for the season, motorists may not have been cautious enough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 2.0 inches of snow in Cedarburg.

One person was killed in a two vehicle accident on icy roads in western Wisconsin. The accident occurred on State Highway 82 in the Township of Jackson (Adams County) where roads were ice covered from freezing rain that was falling in advance of a storm system moving northeast toward the Great Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a two vehicle accident on State Highway 82 in the Township of Jackson. The accident occurred as a result of icy roads from freezing rain that was occurring at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations up to two tenths of an inch from freezing rain were reported across western Wisconsin from a winter storm that impacted the region on January 27th. The winter storm originated over South Dakota and then moved almost due east into Wisconsin. As it did, it pulled a warm front north through the region. The warm air that came north with the warm front was responsible for the freezing rain. The warm air moved as far north as about Interstate 90, which is where sleet began mixing in with the freezing rain. Farther to the north, all the precipitation fell as snow. The freezing rain and sleet produced a coating of ice on most exposed surfaces leading to very slick roads with several accidents occurring. The highest reported ice accumulation was two tenths of an inch in Prairie du Chien (Crawford County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and sleet created a coating of ice on exposed surfaces leading to very slick roads and numerous accidents occurred across the county. In addition to the freezing rain and sleet, snow also occurred. The highest reported snowfall amount was 2.8 inches in Osseo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures well above freezing for two days combined with 1.5 to 2.5 inches of rain produced some minor river flooding along the Platte and Grant Rivers in Grant County. The flooding closed a few township roads January 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snow melt and heavy rains pushed the Platte River out of its banks near Rockville. The river crested almost a foot and a half above the flood stage at 10.37 feet.

Snowfall amounts averaged 14 to 16 inches with 1 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation across western Wisconsin in the counties still affected by the drought. With the frozen ground, only a small amount of this precipitation was absorbed by the soil. Soil conditions improved enough across southern Grant County for that area to be removed from the severe drought. No change in the drought conditions occurred elsewhere and a severe to extreme drought remained across Buffalo, Taylor and Trempealeau Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a half inch of liquid precipitation fell on the southern part of Grant County during the first few days of February. Enough of this moisture was absorbed for improvements in soil moisture conditions and ended the severe drought.

Increasing northwest winds behind the low produced areas of blowing and drifting snow as wind gusts reached 30 to 35 mph (26 to 29 knots).  Numerous accidents and spin-offs were reported by area law enforcement officials.  Meteorologically, deep forcing with sloped frontogenesis and strong layer Q-vector convergence, combined with some convective instability in and above the dendritic growth zone, fueled the moderate to briefly heavy snowfall intensities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A co-operative observer 3 miles south of Berlin reported total snow accumulations of 9.1 inches.

Strong, widespread west to northwest wind gusts in the 45 to 50 mph (39 to 43 knots) range occurred over Southern Wisconsin during the evening of January 19th.  These winds, behind a strong cold front, ushered in the coldest air to that point of the winter season.  Newspaper articles reported scattered power outages as wind-broken tree limbs impacted power-lines.  The strong winds were related to a tightening pressure gradient around a low pressure system that rapidly deepened as it tracked across the Upper Peninsula of Michigan to southwest Quebec.

Rain changed over to snow over most of Southeast Wisconsin and far southeast portions of South-central Wisconsin as low pressure followed a favored track from southern Missouri to eastern Lower Michigan during the day on January 30th.   Snow accumulations between 3 and 5 inches were reported along and southeast of a Rock County to Ozaukee County line.  A slower transition from rain to snow limited total snow accumulations to 1 to 3 inches in Milwaukee...Racine and Kenosha counties.  Increasing northwest winds behind the low pressure produced areas of blowing and drifting snow as wind gusts reached 30 to 35 mph (26 to 29 knots).  Roads glazed over as the standing water from early rains froze when temperatures dropped, which along with the blowing and drifting snow was sufficient, when combined with the accumulating snow, to create hazardous travel conditions.  Numerous accidents and spin-offs were reported by area law enforcement officials, including an injury accident in Jefferson County.  Meteorologically, deep forcing with sloped frontogenesis and strong layer Q-vector convergence, combined with some convective instability in and above the dendritic growth zone, produced moderate to briefly intense snowfall intensities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A co-operative observer located  2 miles south of Watertown reported total snow accumulations of 5.8 inches. The slick driving conditions resulted in three injury accidents in Jefferson County, including a car losing control and causing a head-on collision on Highway 16 in northern Jefferson County. Both drivers in that accident  were taken to nearby hospitals for treatment of non life-threatening injuries (indirectly-related). No information was available for the other two accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow affecting Southern Wisconsin on January 27th became mixed with some freezing rain and sleet which resulted in hazardous driving conditions.  There were brief heavy showers of sleet and bursts of snow with isolated elevated thunderstorms.  The mix of snow...sleet and freezing rain left a glaze 0.1 to 0.2 inch thick on area roads and exposed surfaces...with snow accumulations of 1/2 inch to nearly 2 inches adding to the slick travel conditions.  The precipitation changed over to all rain from south to north during the evening and overnight hours as low-level warm air overspread the region.  The warm air was related to a warm front moving north through the western Great Lake Region.  In fact, about 5,000 feet above the ground there was a 50-knot (58 mph) low-level southerly jet that provide the energy and moisture for the isolated thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air spread into southern Wisconsin behind deep low pressure that tracked to the north of the state.  About 2,000 feet above ground level, 925 mb temperatures fell to -17C to -22C���with 925 mb winds of 20 to 30 knots.  Momentum transfer of these winds to the surface produced gusty northwest winds, which combined with surface temperatures in the single digits below zero to produce wind chills between -20 to -30.  The frigid wind chills began the morning of January 21st across far northern Southeast Wisconsin and most of South-central Wisconsin���then spread across the remainder of southern Wisconsin during the evening of January 21st and continued everywhere into the morning hours of January 22nd.||An interesting side note to this cold outbreak was the fact that it was one of the relatively few times Milwaukee recorded a low temperature below zero without having a snow cover.

Dense fog affected Southern Wisconsin during the early morning hours of January 11th and lasted through the evening hours of the 11th.   Visibilities were reduced to 1/8 to 1/4 mile.  This dense fog was the result of southerly winds ahead of a  low pressure in the Dakotas which pulled higher dew point air and some rain into Southern Wisconsin.  The low-level air was chilled and saturated as it passed over the snow-covered ground.

A line of severe thunderstorms, known as a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS), produced six tornadoes and widespread damaging winds across east-central Wisconsin during the late night hours of August 6-7.  The storms formed ahead of a cold front that also produced severe weather in Minnesota and western Wisconsin.||The tornadoes occurred within about 50 minutes--between 12:22 am and 1:10 am CDT.  Hundreds of homes, businesses and farm buildings were damaged. Two injuries occurred at a campground in Waupaca County.  Thousands of trees and power lines were also downed, leaving tens of thousands of people without power. The strongest tornado of the event reached EF2 intensity (winds estimated at 115 to 120 mph). This tornado caused significant damage to a church in New London. ||The six tornadoes that occurred on August 7 were the most nighttime tornadoes in a single event in northeast Wisconsin since accurate tornado records began (1950).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado that formed in Brown County at 12:59 am CDT continued east-southeast into Kewaunee County, crossing the county line 2.9 miles southwest of Pilsen at 1:06 am CDT. The tornado caused the walls and roof of a barn to collapse (DI 1, DOD 6) and damaged the tops of two silos. In addition, the tornado damaged and uprooted several dozen trees along its path. Average path width in Kewaunee County was 75 yards.

Dense fog affected Southern Wisconsin during the early morning hours of January 11th and lasted through the evening hours of the 11th.   Visibilities were reduced to 1/8 to 1/4 mile.  This dense fog was the result of southerly winds ahead of a  low pressure in the Dakotas which pulled higher dew point air and some rain into Southern Wisconsin.  The low-level air was chilled and saturated as it passed over the snow-covered ground.

Heavy snow fell over most of South-central Wisconsin and northwest portions of Southeast Wisconsin as low pressure followed a favored track from southern Missouri to eastern Lower Michigan during the day on January 30th.  Moderate to briefly intense snowfall occurred with snow accumulations of 6 to 10 inches measured generally along and northwest of a Lafayette County to Sheboygan County line.  Peak snow amounts included 10 in Reedsburg (Sauk Co.) and Poynette (Columbia Co.), 9.1 in Berlin (Green Lake Co.), and 9 in Westfield (Marquette Co.).  Increasing northwest winds behind the low produced areas of blowing and drifting snow as wind gusts reached 30 to 35 mph (26 to 29 knots).  Numerous accidents and spin-offs were reported by area law enforcement officials.  Meteorologically, deep forcing with sloped frontogenesis and strong layer Q-vector convergence, combined with some convective instability in and above the dendritic growth zone, fueled the moderate to briefly heavy snowfall intensities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A co-operative observer in Poynette reported total snow accumulations of 10.0 inches.

Strong, widespread west to northwest wind gusts in the 45 to 50 mph (39 to 43 knots) range occurred over Southern Wisconsin during the evening of January 19th.  These winds, behind a strong cold front, ushered in the coldest air to that point of the winter season.  Newspaper articles reported scattered power outages as wind-broken tree limbs impacted power-lines.  The strong winds were related to a tightening pressure gradient around a low pressure system that rapidly deepened as it tracked across the Upper Peninsula of Michigan to southwest Quebec.

A line of severe thunderstorms, known as a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS), produced six tornadoes and widespread damaging winds across east-central Wisconsin during the late night hours of August 6-7.  The storms formed ahead of a cold front that also produced severe weather in Minnesota and western Wisconsin.||The tornadoes occurred within about 50 minutes--between 12:22 am and 1:10 am CDT.  Hundreds of homes, businesses and farm buildings were damaged. Two injuries occurred at a campground in Waupaca County.  Thousands of trees and power lines were also downed, leaving tens of thousands of people without power. The strongest tornado of the event reached EF2 intensity (winds estimated at 115 to 120 mph). This tornado caused significant damage to a church in New London. ||The six tornadoes that occurred on August 7 were the most nighttime tornadoes in a single event in northeast Wisconsin since accurate tornado records began (1950).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed in eastern Brown County at 12:59 am CDT and moved east-southeast into Kewaunee County. A couple dozen trees were damaged and uprooted (DI 28, DOD 2 and 3) before it moved into Kewaunee County at about 1:06 am CDT, 2.9 miles southwest of Pilsen. Average path width in Brown County was 75 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brisk northwest winds brought bitterly cold arctic air into Wisconsin.  A Green Bay man died of hypothermia outside his home early on the morning of January 21st. Temperatures were in the single digits with wind chills of 10 below to 15 below zero. The man was wearing inadequate clothing for the weather and died from prolonged exposure to the cold, according to Green Bay Police. Preliminary results indicated that alcohol was a factor in his death. The estimated time of death was approximately 2 AM CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite above normal precipitation across north-central Wisconsin western Oneida County remained in the severe drought (D2) category throughout February 2013.

These winds were the related to a low pressure tracking across northern Wisconsin which pulled a strong warm front through southern Wisconsin during the late morning to early afternoon hours.  The resulting low-level mixing allowed downward momentum transfer from a 50-60 knot low-level jet down to the surface.  Newspaper articles reported scattered power outages as wind-broken tree limbs impacted power-lines.

Ice accumulations up to two tenths of an inch from freezing rain were reported across western Wisconsin from a winter storm that impacted the region on January 27th. The winter storm originated over South Dakota and then moved almost due east into Wisconsin. As it did, it pulled a warm front north through the region. The warm air that came north with the warm front was responsible for the freezing rain. The warm air moved as far north as about Interstate 90, which is where sleet began mixing in with the freezing rain. Farther to the north, all the precipitation fell as snow. The freezing rain and sleet produced a coating of ice on most exposed surfaces leading to very slick roads with several accidents occurring. The highest reported ice accumulation was two tenths of an inch in Prairie du Chien (Crawford County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and sleet created a coating of ice on exposed surfaces leading to very slick roads and numerous accidents occurred across the county. The highest reported ice accumulation was a tenth of an inch near Stoddard. In addition to the freezing rain and sleet, snow also occurred. The highest reported snowfall amount was one inch near Hillsboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 5 to 12 inches covered portions of western Wisconsin from a winter storm that moved through the region on January 30th. The storm originated over the southern Plains states and moved northeast through the Mississippi River Valley producing a blanket of heavy snow from central and eastern Iowa into Wisconsin. In addition to the snow, the storm produced northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph that created blowing and drifting snow that at times, lowered the visibility to a quarter mile. The timing of the storm, had a direct impact on the morning commute of the 30th. The highest reported snowfall amount was 11.5 inches in Richland Center (Richland County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow created hazardous conditions across the county, especially during the morning commute time. The visibility at times dropped as low a quarter of a mile in the snow and blowing snow. The highest reported snowfall total was 9.9 inches in Friendship.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The northern end of a snow area from low pressure passing well to the south of southern Wisconsin brought several inches of snow to far southern Wisconsin. Lake effect snow occurred during and after this event with higher snow totals over far southeast Wisconsin. Numerous slide-offs and accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of snow with multiple slide-offs and accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air spread into southern Wisconsin behind deep low pressure that tracked to the north of the state.  About 2,000 feet above ground level, 925 mb temperatures fell to -17C to -22C���with 925 mb winds of 20 to 30 knots.  Momentum transfer of these winds to the surface produced gusty northwest winds, which combined with surface temperatures in the single digits below zero to produce wind chills between -20 to -30.  The frigid wind chills began the morning of January 21st across far northern Southeast Wisconsin and most of South-central Wisconsin���then spread across the remainder of southern Wisconsin during the evening of January 21st and continued everywhere into the morning hours of January 22nd.||An interesting side note to this cold outbreak was the fact that it was one of the relatively few times Milwaukee recorded a low temperature below zero without having a snow cover.

Snow affecting Southern Wisconsin on January 27th became mixed with some freezing rain and sleet which resulted in hazardous driving conditions.  There were brief heavy showers of sleet and bursts of snow with isolated elevated thunderstorms.  The mix of snow...sleet and freezing rain left a glaze 0.1 to 0.2 inch thick on area roads and exposed surfaces...with snow accumulations of 1/2 inch to nearly 2 inches adding to the slick travel conditions.  The precipitation changed over to all rain from south to north during the evening and overnight hours as low-level warm air overspread the region.  The warm air was related to a warm front moving north through the western Great Lake Region.  In fact, about 5,000 feet above the ground there was a 50-knot (58 mph) low-level southerly jet that provide the energy and moisture for the isolated thunderstorms.

A surge of relatively warm and moist air aloft led to the development of light to moderate snow during the morning rush hour with 1-3 inches accumulating. Roads became snow covered and numerous slide-offs and accidents occurred, especially in the Madison area and the Milwaukee Metropolitan area.  Two indirect deaths occurred with vehicle mishaps in Dane County. Since this was the first snow event for the season, motorists may not have been cautious enough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service office 4 miles southeast of Sullivan reported 1.6 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations up to two tenths of an inch from freezing rain were reported across western Wisconsin from a winter storm that impacted the region on January 27th. The winter storm originated over South Dakota and then moved almost due east into Wisconsin. As it did, it pulled a warm front north through the region. The warm air that came north with the warm front was responsible for the freezing rain. The warm air moved as far north as about Interstate 90, which is where sleet began mixing in with the freezing rain. Farther to the north, all the precipitation fell as snow. The freezing rain and sleet produced a coating of ice on most exposed surfaces leading to very slick roads with several accidents occurring. The highest reported ice accumulation was two tenths of an inch in Prairie du Chien (Crawford County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and sleet created a coating of ice on exposed surfaces leading to very slick roads and numerous accidents occurred across the county. In addition to the freezing rain and sleet, snow also occurred. The highest reported snowfall amount was 1.4 inches near Warrens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of 3 to 4 inches were measured mainly along and south of a Wisconsin Dells to Madison to Lake Geneva line.  The snow fell during the overnight hours of February 2nd into the morning commute hours of February 3rd and resulted in the usual dozens of vehicle accidents due to slippery and/or icy roads.  The snow as generated by an Alberta Clipper-type short wave trough which tracked across Wisconsin.  Strong isentropic omega and sloped frontogenetic forcing along with relatively high snow to liquid ratios between 18:1 and 23:1 made this system a very efficient snow maker.
