A winter storm affected parts of South-central and Southeast Wisconsin with steady moderate to heavy snow.  Snowfall accumulations of 8 to 12 inches were reported over the eastern counties of Southeast Wisconsin...with the lake-influence clearly noted by the higher 12 to 18 inch accumulations where the landmass extends out into the lake around Wind Point in Racine County and eastern Sheboygan County. Oostburg, in southeast Sheboygan County, recorded 18.6 inches of snow. The rapidly accumulating heavy snows made it difficult for plowing crews to keep area roadways passable, causing closures of county roads. The rapid accumulation of snow made it difficult to keep roads clear, even in the larger cities in the east, where the snow intensity was enhanced by convergent flow along the Lake Michigan shoreline.  The most rapid accumulations occurred during the early evening of February 26th, and continued overnight into the morning hours of February 27th.||This winter storm was due to a deformation zone on the north side of low pressure that tracked from Missouri, across Illinois to Lower Michigan.  The deformation zone lifted north through southern Wisconsin during the late afternoon and evening hours of February 26th, then stalled in an east-west fashion over East-Central Wisconsin during the overnight hours, with an easterly flow feeding additional lake moisture into the system.  Later on the deformation zone rotated to a more north-south line and dropped southeast through Southeast Wisconsin during the early morning of February 27th as the low pulled away to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall over Washington County ranged from 6 to around 10 inches. A snow total of 9.6 inches was reported in West Bend.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another winter weather event affected parts of South-Central and Southeast Wisconsin inland away from Lake Michigan.  Steady snow, at a moderate to at times heavy intensity, accumulated during the early evening of February 26th through the morning hours of February 27th.  Snowfall accumulations ranged from 3 to 7 inches, and the usual dozens of vehicle accidents occurred.  This winter weather event was related to a Winter Storm event that affected Southeast Wisconsin counties near Lake Michigan with a snow accumulations of 8 to 18 inches.  The responsible low pressure system for the winter weather event and winter storm event tracked from Missouri across Illinois to Lower Michigan.

A winter weather event deposited snow totals of 3 to 7 inches across parts of South-Central and Southeast Wisconsin on February 7th.  The precipitation started out as a mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow early in the morning and changed to all snow by mid-morning.  The greatest snow accumulations were reached during the evening commute, creating hazardous travel conditions.  Some of the higher snow totals included 6.8 inches in the Town of Westford in Dodge County and 6.1 inches 1 mile south of Columbus in Columbia County.  The responsible low pressure system came out of the Southern Plains the morning of the 7th and intensified as it crossed southern Illinois and central Indiana during the day and into the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts averaged 14 to 20 inches with 1.75 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation across the western Wisconsin counties still affected by the drought. With the frozen ground, little if any of the precipitation was absorbed by the soil. No changes in the drought conditions occurred and a severe drought remained across parts of Buffalo, Taylor and Trempealeau Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across Buffalo County averaged 20 to 22 inches with 2.75 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation during March. With the frozen ground, little to none of this moisture was absorbed by the soil. Only minor changes in the drought conditions occurred and all but the extreme southern tip of the county remained in a severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elderly western Wisconsin woman wandered away from a care facility in Viroqua (Vernon County) and died of hypothermia from exposure to the cold. The automated weather observing equipment at the Viroqua airport on March 7th reported temperatures in the teens with an overnight low of 12 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 93 year old lady passed away due to hypothermia from exposure to the cold after wandering away from a Viroqua care facility. The automated weather observing equipment at the Viroqua airport reported temperatures in the teens with an overnight low on the 7th of 12 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South-Central and Southeast Wisconsin picked up 3 to 4 inches of snow due to yet another winter weather event.  This snow was accompanied by east winds gusting to 30 mph which caused some blowing and minor drifting of snow that reduced visibilities.  The responsible low pressure moved northeast from the Southern Plains to Missouri the night of February 21st and across southern Wisconsin during the day on February 22nd.
Severe Drought (D2) conditions continued from far Western Douglas County, all of Burnett county, the southern half of Washburn County, extreme southern Sawyer County and most of Price County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued across Central and Southern Price County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm affected parts of South-central and Southeast Wisconsin with steady moderate to heavy snow.  Snowfall accumulations of 8 to 12 inches were reported over the eastern counties of Southeast Wisconsin...with the lake-influence clearly noted by the higher 12 to 18 inch accumulations where the landmass extends out into the lake around Wind Point in Racine County and eastern Sheboygan County. Oostburg, in southeast Sheboygan County, recorded 18.6 inches of snow. The rapidly accumulating heavy snows made it difficult for plowing crews to keep area roadways passable, causing closures of county roads. The rapid accumulation of snow made it difficult to keep roads clear, even in the larger cities in the east, where the snow intensity was enhanced by convergent flow along the Lake Michigan shoreline.  The most rapid accumulations occurred during the early evening of February 26th, and continued overnight into the morning hours of February 27th.||This winter storm was due to a deformation zone on the north side of low pressure that tracked from Missouri, across Illinois to Lower Michigan.  The deformation zone lifted north through southern Wisconsin during the late afternoon and evening hours of February 26th, then stalled in an east-west fashion over East-Central Wisconsin during the overnight hours, with an easterly flow feeding additional lake moisture into the system.  Later on the deformation zone rotated to a more north-south line and dropped southeast through Southeast Wisconsin during the early morning of February 27th as the low pulled away to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall across the county ranged from 8 to 10 inches in the western half to 12 to around 19 inches in the east. 18.6 inches of snow was reported in Oostburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system began to organize across the northern Rockies Sunday night, March 3rd. A band of light snow developed ahead of this system across western Minnesota, and moved east across the state Sunday night. Much drier air across the western Great Lakes kept most of the snowfall very light or non-measurable across far eastern Minnesota until sunrise as the atmosphere moistened up. The continued dry air to the east eventually caused the light snow to dissipate across western Wisconsin, and then turn over to flurries by the afternoon. By the late afternoon, and into the evening of Monday, March 4th, the storm system became more organized, and moved southeast across the eastern Dakotas.  This allowed for bands of moderate snowfall to develop across eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin. The snow tapered off Tuesday afternoon March 5th, but as the storm wound down, a band of 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across far west central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather observers across the county reported between 6 to 10 inches of snowfall that started early Monday afternoon, and lasted through midday Tuesday, March 5th.

In the southeast part of the county, amounts were less than 3 inches, but sleet and freezing rain accumulated up to 0.10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a car accident after the driver lost control on snow and ice covered roads near Viroqua (Vernon County) on the morning of the March 11th. Almost a foot of heavy wet snow fell on parts of western Wisconsin from a winter storm on March 10th and 11th. The storm responsible for this snow moved northeast from the Kansas City region to Lake Michigan. Along with the snow, the system also produced northwest winds of 15 to 20 mph that created some drifting of the snow. Numerous schools were either closed or utilized a two hour delay on the 11th. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 10 inches were common and the highest reported total was 10 inches in Longwood (Clark County). Around 600 customers across Buffalo and Trempealeau Counties lost power because of the storm from the late evening of the 10th through the morning of the 11th. Ahead of the snowfall, the storm produced up to 1.5 inches of rain with temperatures in the upper 30s. This produced abundant run off from the rain and melting snow that was not able to penetrate into the ground due to frost depths up to 3 feet. The run off flooded basements and caused numerous roads to be covered with standing water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer and cooperative observers reported between 4 and 10 inches of snow across the county. The highest reported total was 10 inches in Longwood.

Snowfall amounts averaged 14 to 20 inches with 1.75 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation across the western Wisconsin counties still affected by the drought. No changes in the drought conditions occurred and a severe drought remained across parts of Buffalo, Taylor and Trempealeau Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across Trempealeau County averaged 16 to 20 inches with 2.25 to 2.75 inches of liquid precipitation during March. With the frozen ground, little to none of this moisture was absorbed by the soil. No changes in the drought conditions occurred and the northern half of the county remained in a severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of melting snow and several rounds of rainfall pushed the Trempealeau River at Dodge (Trempealeau County) out of its banks three times in April between the 1st and 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of melting snow and several rounds of rain caused the Trempealeau River in Dodge to go above flood stage three times between April 1st and April 19th. The first round of flooding occurred on the 1st and 2nd with a crest of 9.21 feet on the 1st. The second round of flooding occurred between the 5th and 14th with a crest of 10.74 feet on the 11th. The final round of flooding occurred between the 16th and 19th with a crest of 9.95 feet on the 17th. Flood stage at Dodge is 9 feet.

Yet another winter weather event affected parts of South-Central and Southeast Wisconsin inland away from Lake Michigan.  Steady snow, at a moderate to at times heavy intensity, accumulated during the early evening of February 26th through the morning hours of February 27th.  Snowfall accumulations ranged from 3 to 7 inches, and the usual dozens of vehicle accidents occurred.  This winter weather event was related to a Winter Storm event that affected Southeast Wisconsin counties near Lake Michigan with a snow accumulations of 8 to 18 inches.  The responsible low pressure system for the winter weather event and winter storm event tracked from Missouri across Illinois to Lower Michigan.

A winter storm affected parts of South-central and Southeast Wisconsin with steady moderate to heavy snow.  Snowfall accumulations of 8 to 12 inches were reported over the eastern counties of Southeast Wisconsin...with the lake-influence clearly noted by the higher 12 to 18 inch accumulations where the landmass extends out into the lake around Wind Point in Racine County and eastern Sheboygan County. Oostburg, in southeast Sheboygan County, recorded 18.6 inches of snow. The rapidly accumulating heavy snows made it difficult for plowing crews to keep area roadways passable, causing closures of county roads. The rapid accumulation of snow made it difficult to keep roads clear, even in the larger cities in the east, where the snow intensity was enhanced by convergent flow along the Lake Michigan shoreline.  The most rapid accumulations occurred during the early evening of February 26th, and continued overnight into the morning hours of February 27th.||This winter storm was due to a deformation zone on the north side of low pressure that tracked from Missouri, across Illinois to Lower Michigan.  The deformation zone lifted north through southern Wisconsin during the late afternoon and evening hours of February 26th, then stalled in an east-west fashion over East-Central Wisconsin during the overnight hours, with an easterly flow feeding additional lake moisture into the system.  Later on the deformation zone rotated to a more north-south line and dropped southeast through Southeast Wisconsin during the early morning of February 27th as the low pulled away to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall over Racine County ranged from 6 inches in western sections to around 14 inches in the far east. A snow total of 13.5 inches was reported in the City of Racine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several township and county roads were closed across Jackson County as a result of flooding. The flooding resulted from ice jams, runoff from melting snow and several rounds of rain. Some of the road closures only lasted a couple of days, while others persisted for weeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Highway P in the Town of Stark was closed from April 10th into the 11th due to flooding along the Kickapoo River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter weather event deposited snow totals of 3 to 7 inches across parts of South-Central and Southeast Wisconsin on February 7th.  The precipitation started out as a mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow early in the morning and changed to all snow by mid-morning.  The greatest snow accumulations were reached during the evening commute, creating hazardous travel conditions.  Some of the higher snow totals included 6.8 inches in the Town of Westford in Dodge County and 6.1 inches 1 mile south of Columbus in Columbia County.  The responsible low pressure system came out of the Southern Plains the morning of the 7th and intensified as it crossed southern Illinois and central Indiana during the day and into the evening.

Snowfall accumulations of 3 to 4 inches were measured mainly along and south of a Wisconsin Dells to Madison to Lake Geneva line.  The snow fell during the overnight hours of February 2nd into the morning commute hours of February 3rd and resulted in the usual dozens of vehicle accidents due to slippery and/or icy roads.  The snow as generated by an Alberta Clipper-type short wave trough which tracked across Wisconsin.  Strong isentropic omega and sloped frontogenetic forcing along with relatively high snow to liquid ratios between 18:1 and 23:1 made this system a very efficient snow maker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system began to organize across the northern Rockies Sunday night, March 3rd. A band of light snow developed ahead of this system across western Minnesota, and moved east across the state Sunday night. Much drier air across the western Great Lakes kept most of the snowfall very light or non-measurable across far eastern Minnesota until sunrise as the atmosphere moistened up. The continued dry air to the east eventually caused the light snow to dissipate across western Wisconsin, and then turn over to flurries by the afternoon. By the late afternoon, and into the evening of Monday, March 4th, the storm system became more organized, and moved southeast across the eastern Dakotas.  This allowed for bands of moderate snowfall to develop across eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin. The snow tapered off Tuesday afternoon March 5th, but as the storm wound down, a band of 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across far west central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather observers across the county reported between 6 to 8 inches of snowfall that started early Monday afternoon, and lasted through midday Tuesday, March 5th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thawing soil and above normal precipitation across north central Wisconsin resulted in a reclassification of the drought in southwest Vilas County from severe drought (D2) to moderate drought (D1) by the morning of April 16th.

A strengthening area of low pressure tracked from eastern Iowa northeast to Lower Michigan. Cold air poured into the region behind the storm system on gusty northwest winds and changed rain into snow. The combination of snowfall from the low pressure system and subsequent lake effect brought over a foot of accumulation to northwest Vilas County. The town of Winchester reported 13.0 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS cooperative observer measured 13.0 inches of snow in the town of Winchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 3 to 6 inches, combined with north to northeast winds of 10 to 20 mph gusting to between 25 and 30 mph, created hazardous travel conditions over portions of Southeast Wisconsin on March 5th. With the snow starting early in the morning and not ending until the mid to late evening hours, the difficult driving conditions covered both the morning and evening rush hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer in Berlin reported 4.0 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One to four inches of snow fell over southern Wisconsin during the morning and afternoon hours of March 18th with strong low pressure passing through the region. The tight surface pressure gradient around the departing low produced west winds of 20 to 35 mph, making driving conditions difficult with reduced visibilities and snow covered roads due to blowing and drifting of the snow that fell earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials indicated hazardous driving conditions developed due to  blowing and drifting snow from gusty west winds during the overnight hours of the 18th into the morning hours of the 19th that lofted the 1 to 3 inches of snow that had fallen during the day on the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that developed across western Wisconsin in the summer of 2012 finally ended during the first part of April. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 4 inches fell over the first half of the month with most of this being absorbed by the soil. This was enough moisture for the severe drought across portions of Buffalo, Taylor and Trempealeau Counties to be downgraded to a moderate drought or abnormally dry. Farmers across the country were expected to receive about $16 billion in crop insurance payouts because of the drought that ruined crops in 2012.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 4 inches of precipitation fell across Buffalo County during the first half of the month. With most of this moisture being absorbed by the soil, the severe drought that formed in the summer of 2012 was downgraded to a moderate drought or abnormally dry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a car accident after the driver lost control on snow and ice covered roads near Viroqua (Vernon County) on the morning of the March 11th. Almost a foot of heavy wet snow fell on parts of western Wisconsin from a winter storm on March 10th and 11th. The storm responsible for this snow moved northeast from the Kansas City region to Lake Michigan. Along with the snow, the system also produced northwest winds of 15 to 20 mph that created some drifting of the snow. Numerous schools were either closed or utilized a two hour delay on the 11th. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 10 inches were common and the highest reported total was 10 inches in Longwood (Clark County). Around 600 customers across Buffalo and Trempealeau Counties lost power because of the storm from the late evening of the 10th through the morning of the 11th. Ahead of the snowfall, the storm produced up to 1.5 inches of rain with temperatures in the upper 30s. This produced abundant run off from the rain and melting snow that was not able to penetrate into the ground due to frost depths up to 3 feet. The run off flooded basements and caused numerous roads to be covered with standing water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a one care accident when the driver lost control on a snow and ice covered road and the vehicle flipped over in a ditch and then hit a tree. Volunteer and cooperative observers reported 1 and 3 inches of snow across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to northwestern Wisconsin near Lake Superior on April 11th and 12th.  Much of the northern parts of Douglas, Bayfield, Ashland, and Iron counties received 8 to 13 inches of snow, and there were isolated areas that received much more.  Snowfall reports include 18.8 inches near the city of Superior, 15 inches near Cornucopia, 12.4 inches at Madeline Island, and 10.5 inches near Upson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Icy conditions developed across portions of western Wisconsin as freezing rain created a layer of ice on some roads and most exposed surfaces. The Buffalo County Emergency Manager reported dangerous road conditions across the county with numerous slide offs. Most schools across Buffalo County were closed April 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy conditions developed across Buffalo County as freezing rain created a layer of ice on some roads and most exposed surfaces. Dangerous road conditions were reported across the county with numerous slide offs. Most schools across Buffalo County were closed or delayed on April 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One to four inches of snow fell over southern Wisconsin during the morning and afternoon hours of March 18th with strong low pressure passing through the region. The tight surface pressure gradient around the departing low produced west winds of 20 to 35 mph, making driving conditions difficult with reduced visibilities and snow covered roads due to blowing and drifting of the snow that fell earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials indicated hazardous driving conditions developed due to  blowing and drifting snow from gusty west winds during the overnight hours of the 18th into the morning hours of the 19th that lofted the 1 to 2 inches of snow that had fallen during the day on the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms that developed across southeast Minnesota, moved quickly northeast into west central Wisconsin, and produced damaging wind gusts from near Eau Claire, to Fairchild, Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down east of Eau Claire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a car accident after the driver lost control on snow and ice covered roads near Viroqua (Vernon County) on the morning of the March 11th. Almost a foot of heavy wet snow fell on parts of western Wisconsin from a winter storm on March 10th and 11th. The storm responsible for this snow moved northeast from the Kansas City region to Lake Michigan. Along with the snow, the system also produced northwest winds of 15 to 20 mph that created some drifting of the snow. Numerous schools were either closed or utilized a two hour delay on the 11th. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 10 inches were common and the highest reported total was 10 inches in Longwood (Clark County). Around 600 customers across Buffalo and Trempealeau Counties lost power because of the storm from the late evening of the 10th through the morning of the 11th. Ahead of the snowfall, the storm produced up to 1.5 inches of rain with temperatures in the upper 30s. This produced abundant run off from the rain and melting snow that was not able to penetrate into the ground due to frost depths up to 3 feet. The run off flooded basements and caused numerous roads to be covered with standing water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snow melt and heavy rains pushed the Platte River out of its banks near Rockville. The river crested almost a foot above the flood stage at 9.98 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to northwestern Wisconsin near Lake Superior on April 11th and 12th.  Much of the northern parts of Douglas, Bayfield, Ashland, and Iron counties received 8 to 13 inches of snow, and there were isolated areas that received much more.  Snowfall reports include 18.8 inches near the city of Superior, 15 inches near Cornucopia, 12.4 inches at Madeline Island, and 10.5 inches near Upson.

The seasons first severe thunderstorms developed during the evening hours of April 9th. These storms were north of a warm front that was draped across central Iowa. The storms produced large hail across southern La Crosse County with up to quarter sized hail falling on the south side of the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County). A car dealership on the south side of La Crosse was hit by the hail with an estimated 170 cars receiving minor dents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported southeast of the city of La Crosse.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of several rounds of rain and melting snow caused the Black River in Black River Falls to go above flood stage three times between April 9th and April 20th. The first round of flooding occurred between the 9th and 12th with a crest of 51.02 feet on the 10th. The second round of flooding occurred on the 15th and 16th with a crest of 49.25 feet on the 16th. The final round of flooding occurred on the 19th and 20th with a crest of 49.15 feet on the 19th. Flood stage at Black River Falls is 47 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two-day rainfall totals of between 0.90 inch and 1.35 inches fell over southern Wisconsin on March 10th through March 11th. This rain fell on frozen ground that also had snow cover between 5 and 12 inches deep, melting some of the snow that added between 0.20 of an inch to nearly 2 inches of water to the rainfall, with all the resulting runoff flowing into area rivers and streams. This pushed several of the rivers into minor to moderate flooding, inundating nearby land areas and roads. Rivers crested between 2 and 4 feet above flood stage. Some of the fast-responding rivers and streams fell back within their banks in a day or two, while others remained above flood stage for almost a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sheboygan near Sheboygan crested at 11.54 feet, 3.54 feet above flood stage, at 805 pm CDT on March 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Less than three days after heavy snow fell across northwest Vilas county, yet another storm hit the northwest part of the county with heavy, wet snow. A cold front moved into central Wisconsin and stalled. An area of low pressure rode northeast along the front and produced snow across north-central Wisconsin. Local law enforcement estimated that eight inches of snow had fallen in the town of Manitowish Waters.

An area of low pressure over the Central Plains moved east along a stationary front and generated a large area of mixed freezing rain, sleet and snow to parts of northeast Wisconsin. There was enough instability present to allow thunder to occur which helped to enhance the ice and snow, mainly over central and east-central Wisconsin. In Appleton, ice accumulations reached at least one-half inch. Numerous trees, branches and power lines were toppled by the weight of the ice. Power outages affected nearly 30,000 customers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated one-half inch of ice fell overnight which led to icing on tree branches and poles. The combination of ice and wind caused some branches to break off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the Central Plains moved east along a stationary front and generated a large area of mixed freezing rain, sleet and snow to parts of northeast Wisconsin. There was enough instability present to allow thunder to occur which helped to enhance the ice and snow, mainly over central and east-central Wisconsin. In Appleton, ice accumulations reached at least one-half inch. Numerous trees, branches and power lines were toppled by the weight of the ice. Power outages affected nearly 30,000 customers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous branches were knocked down due to heavy icing. The largest branches measured 10 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small tornado crossed the Mississippi River from Iowa hitting a campground just north of Bagley (Grant County). This tornado was part of a larger severe weather outbreak that occurred across much of western Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of May 29th. These storms developed in a warm and moist airmass behind a warm front that was moving north across the Upper Midwest. In addition to the tornado, a pole barn and other structures were damaged near Whitehall (Trempealeau County) and structural damage to buildings also occurred near Richland Center (Richland County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South of Richland Center, buildings and trees were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms rolled across western Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of May 19th. The first round of storms produced wind damage across Buffalo County during the afternoon. The second round of storms in the evening produced additional severe wind gusts in Vernon, Grant and Richland Counties. The most significant damage occurred in Gilmanton (Buffalo County) were a dozen power poles were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around a dozen power poles were blown down in Gilmanton.

Some trees were felled by the weight of the accumulated ice combined with strong northeast winds gusting to around 35 mph. The freezing rain and sleet were on the northern edge of a large precipitation shield associated with persistent warm air advection aloft, with the rain falling into a sub-freezing layer near the ground.

For the second straight day, thunderstorms developed during the afternoon across western Wisconsin. Some of these storms produced damaging winds from central Grant County northeast into Adams County. A wind gust of 70 mph occurred near Lancaster (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down west of Gotham near the Grant County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast developing snow storm quickly developed across western Iowa Monday afternoon, April 22nd, and moved across northern Iowa, and into central Wisconsin by early Tuesday morning.  A mixture of rain and snow occurred over south central and east central Minnesota, as well as west central Wisconsin during the afternoon, but it quickly changed over to all snow by the early evening and became heavy at times. The mixture of rain and snow held on through 11 pm around Eau Claire, Menomonie and Chippewa Falls which kept totals under 1-3 inches. The heaviest snow fell along a narrow band from Spring Valley, northeast to Clear Lake, Rice Lake and Haugen.  These areas received between 5 to 7 inches, with locally 10 inches near Comstock and Almena in western Barron County. The snow tapered to flurries after 2 am and then ended around 5 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 7 inches across the county with the heaviest near Spring Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second week of April saw rainfall totals between 2 inches and 5 inches across southern Wisconsin. These totals represented 300 percent to 600 percent of the amount normally seen from April 7th through the 14th.  This pushed several of the rivers into minor to moderate flooding, with major flooding at several locations on the Rock River, and on the Fox River at New Munster, inundating nearby land areas and roads. Rivers crested between 1 foot and 4.5 feet above flood stage. Runoff was enhanced by a lingering deep frost layer. While the top 4 or 5 inches of soil had thawed, there was still a foot or more of frost below the top-most unfrozen layer, preventing much of the rain from percolating into the ground. Some of the fast-responding rivers and streams fell back within their banks in a day or two, while others remained above flood stage through the end of the month and into May as rain totals for the entire month ranged between 5 and 8 inches, or between 150 percent and 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fox River at New Munster went into major flood, cresting at 14.5 feet, or 4.5 feet over the flood stage of 10 feet, at 1245 AM CDT on April 20th.The rier remained above flood stage into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved east across southern Wisconsin during the evening hours of May 28th. The storms produced large hail in southern Waukesha County. A surface low developed northeastward into the Upper Great Lakes along a warm front that was moving northward through the region. A broad southwesterly low-level jet brought  increasingly moist boundary layer air into the region that impinged on the warm front, providing the forcing that triggered the line of thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported hail up to 1.25 inches in diameter about 1 mile southwest of Mukwonago.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unusual snow event unfolded across portions of south central and east central Minnesota, as well as west central Wisconsin on the evening of Wednesday, May 1st. Snowfall amounts topped over a foot of snow, with locally 12 to 15 inches in a small band near River Falls, Baldwin, Rice Lake and Ladysmith, Wisconsin, by the time the snow tapered off early Thursday afternoon, May 2nd. ||This system was highly unusual due to the fact that May snowfall amounts average less than an inch. A strong Canadian air mass for early May allowed for critical temperatures to lower enough to produce snow across south central and east central Minnesota during the late afternoon and early evening of Wednesday May 1st. Areas around Chippewa Falls, and Eau Clairie, Wisconsin, were warmer initially, which kept this area in a mixture of sleet and rain, before changing over to all snow by Thursday morning, May 2nd. ||A strong surge of moisture from the gulf interacted with the cold air mass to produce very heavy snowfall from Wednesday evening, through Thursday morning, before tapering off after 12 pm. Snowfall rates were as high as 1-2 inches per hour during the height of the storm. The following are some of the higher snowfall totals, ending Thursday afternoon, May 2nd. ||Near Ladysmith 15.0 inches|Rice Lake 14.0 inches. |Weyerhaeuser 14.0 inches. |River Falls 13.0 inches. |Baldwin 12.0 inches. |Jim Falls 11.4 inches. |Durand 10.5 inches. ||Both Ladysmith, and River Falls, broke the 24 hour, calendar day state record of 12.0 inches for the most snowfall in May.  Both these sites beat the previous Wisconsin state daily record in May of 10.9 inches.   ||Due to the heavy snowfall and high liquid water content, large tree branches broke off and caused damage to power lines, along with numerous power outages. ||Highland Ridge Campground at the Eau Galle Dam and Recreation Site, located in Spring Valley, Wisconsin, had excessive storm damage due to the extremely wet, and heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 13 inches across the county with the heaviest near Spring Valley and River Falls, which both measured 13.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms began to develop across south central Minnesota during the afternoon of Sunday, May 19th.  These storms started to accelerate northward near Owatonna, southeast to Blooming Prairie where the first report of damage was reported northwest of the town. As these storms started to accelerate to the north-northeast, several bow-echo type thunderstorm structures moved across east central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin during the early evening. Reports of 60 mph winds, trees blown down, damaged barns and power outages occurred from two lines of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down north of Ellsworth, Wisconsin.

Areas around Chippewa Falls, and Eau Clairie, Wisconsin, were warmer initially, which kept this area in a mixture of sleet and rain, before changing over to all snow by Thursday morning, May 2nd. ||A strong surge of moisture from the gulf interacted with the cold air mass to produce very heavy snowfall from Wednesday evening, through Thursday morning, before tapering off after 12 pm. Snowfall rates were as high as 1-2 inches per hour during the height of the storm. The following are some of the higher snowfall totals, ending Thursday afternoon, May 2nd. ||Near Ladysmith 15.0 inches|Rice Lake 14.0 inches. |Weyerhaeuser 14.0 inches. |River Falls 13.0 inches. |Baldwin 12.0 inches. |Jim Falls 11.4 inches. |Durand 10.5 inches. ||Both Ladysmith, and River Falls, broke the 24 hour, calendar day state record of 12.0 inches for the most snowfall in May.  Both these sites beat the previous Wisconsin state daily record in May of 10.9 inches.   ||Due to the heavy snowfall and high liquid water content, large tree branches broke off and caused damage to power lines, along with numerous power outages. ||Highland Ridge Campground at the Eau Galle Dam and Recreation Site, located in Spring Valley, Wisconsin, had excessive storm damage due to the extremely wet, and heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 7 to 10 inches across the county with the heaviest around Eau Claire.  In Eau Claire, 8.7 inches fell on May 2nd, which broke the record May snowfall at the WQOW TV.  In addition, Eau Claire Airport had a record daily cold high temperature for the day of 34 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of showers and some thunderstorms during the second half of April brought rain totals for the entire month to between 5 and 8 inches, or between 150 percent and 200 percent of normal across southern Wisconsin. With some rivers already in flood from the 2 inches to 5 inches of rain that had fallen across southern Wisconsin earlier in the month, additional minor to moderate flooding occurred at locations that had either just fallen below flood stage from the early month rain, or went into flood with the added amounts in the second half of the month. Rivers crested between 1 and 2 feet above flood stage, inundating nearby land areas and roads.  Some of the fast-responding rivers and streams fell back within their banks in a day or two, while others remained above flood stage for almost a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East Branch of the Pecatonica River near Martintown crested at 15.11 feet, or 1.61 feet above the flood stage of 13.5 feet at 1145 PM on April 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced lightning during the afternoon hours of April 9th and again during the early morning hours of April 10th, igniting 3 separate house fires. The lightning also stuck power lines that caused power outages affecting about 6300 customers in the Milwaukee metropolitan area. The thunderstorms fired up as a low-level jet impinged along an 850 mb baroclinic zone that set up over the area ahead of a 500 mb short-wave, with additional forcing for deep vertical motions provided by a nearly-coupled upper jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house fire occurred in the town of New Berlin when the residence was struck by lightning. Only minor damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wake low developed behind an area of rain showers that moved through the region in the early morning hours of April 11th. There were reports of downed tree branches and scattered power outages due to downed power lines, as well as entire trees being downed by the powerful wind gusts that reached as high as 52 mph (45 knots) at Watertown Airport.

Thunderstorms began to develop across south central Minnesota during the afternoon of Sunday, May 19th.  These storms started to accelerate northward near Owatonna, southeast to Blooming Prairie where the first report of damage was reported northwest of the town. As these storms started to accelerate to the north-northeast, several bow-echo type thunderstorm structures moved across east central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin during the early evening. Reports of 60 mph winds, trees blown down, damaged barns and power outages occurred from two lines of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two barns were damaged, shingles were blown off four houses, along with several small trees uprooted between Baldwin and Hammond, Wisconsin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second week of April saw rainfall totals between 2 inches and 5 inches across southern Wisconsin. These totals represented 300 percent to 600 percent of the amount normally seen from April 7th through the 14th.  This pushed several of the rivers into minor to moderate flooding, with major flooding at several locations on the Rock River, and on the Fox River at New Munster, inundating nearby land areas and roads. Rivers crested between 1 foot and 4.5 feet above flood stage. Runoff was enhanced by a lingering deep frost layer. While the top 4 or 5 inches of soil had thawed, there was still a foot or more of frost below the top-most unfrozen layer, preventing much of the rain from percolating into the ground. Some of the fast-responding rivers and streams fell back within their banks in a day or two, while others remained above flood stage through the end of the month and into May as rain totals for the entire month ranged between 5 and 8 inches, or between 150 percent and 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rock River near Afton experienced moderate flooding, cresting at 11.76 feet, or 2.76 feet over the flood stage of 9 feet, at 10 AM CDT on April 18th. The river remained above flood stage into May.

Several rounds of thunderstorms rolled across western Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of May 19th. The first round of storms produced wind damage across Buffalo County during the afternoon. The second round of storms in the evening produced additional severe wind gusts in Vernon, Grant and Richland Counties. The most significant damage occurred in Gilmanton (Buffalo County) were a dozen power poles were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down south of Cochrane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system began to organize across the southern Rockies early April 17th, and moved quickly northeast across the Plains, and into the Upper Midwest by Thursday evening, April 18th. This was a typically Oklahoma Hooker which originated in southern Rockies, gained strength in the southern Plains, and moved rapidly north-northeast across Oklahoma, Missouri, Iowa, and into northern Wisconsin. ||Initially, east central and southeast Minnesota, as well as west central Wisconsin were warm enough to see mostly sleet and rain during the onset of precipitation Thursday morning. As the system moved across southern Wisconsin, and into northern Wisconsin by the afternoon and evening, colder air changed the precipitation over to all snow from eastern Minnesota, into western Wisconsin.  ||As the storm intensified Thursday afternoon, the snow became heavy at times across central and south central Minnesota, with the sleet and snow, transitioning into all snow by the late afternoon across the Twin Cities, eastern Metro.  By the evening, most of eastern Minnesota had snow, with sleet and snow continuing across west central Wisconsin until late evening where the mixture changed over to all snow. ||Snowfall rates during the late afternoon, and evening hours of Thursday, April 18th, were one to two inches per hour, with isolated three inch per hour rates.  Snowfall amounts quickly increased during the evening, with several areas already receiving 6 or more inches before 9 pm. ||By Friday morning, as the snow tapered off to occasional flurries and snow showers, snowfall amounts ranged from several inches in far southeast Polk County, to locally a foot near Luck and Cushing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 6 inches across the southeast part of the county, up to 12 inches in the northwest part near Cushing and Luck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several lines of severe thunderstorms crossed southern Wisconsin during the evening hours of May 14th. The storms produced damaging wind gusts up to 70 to 75 mph (60 to 65 knots). Widespread damage was reported in swaths up to 6 miles in width, which included structural damage to homes and farm buildings as well as downed trees and power lines due to the powerful winds. WE Energies reported 23,000 customers in Dodge, Jefferson, Waukesha and Milwaukee counties were without power at the height of the storm. The severe thunderstorms formed along a warm front that extended from weak low pressure in northwest Wisconsin south of Superior to near Chicago. While wind shear and instability were marginal, evaporative cooling as the rain fell into dry air in the lower levels produced prime conditions for downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newspaper articles indicated numerous trees and power lines downed by thunderstorm wind gusts, estimated at 70 mph (61 knots) along a path from 2 miles north of Portland to 3 miles east of Ashippun.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in moist and unstable air ahead of an approaching front.  Some of the storms produced large hail and strong wind gusts.  The largest hail fell in Oneida County, where quarter to golf ball size hail fell in the Rhinelander area and quarter size hail fell near Lake Tomahawk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced quarter size hail at Rhinelander.

The damage, in a 6-mile wide swath from 2 miles south of Waterloo to 2.5 miles east of Ixonia, included the destruction of a large pole barn, which had 4-inch by 4-inch support posts ripped out of the ground by the winds.  Other homes, barns and sheds were reported to be damaged by the severe thunderstorm wind gusts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees near Maple Road and U.S. Highway 45.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several lines of severe thunderstorms crossed southern Wisconsin during the evening hours of May 14th. The storms produced damaging wind gusts up to 70 to 75 mph (60 to 65 knots). Widespread damage was reported in swaths up to 6 miles in width, which included structural damage to homes and farm buildings as well as downed trees and power lines due to the powerful winds. WE Energies reported 23,000 customers in Dodge, Jefferson, Waukesha and Milwaukee counties were without power at the height of the storm. The severe thunderstorms formed along a warm front that extended from weak low pressure in northwest Wisconsin south of Superior to near Chicago. While wind shear and instability were marginal, evaporative cooling as the rain fell into dry air in the lower levels produced prime conditions for downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported numerous trees and power lines downed by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated up to 70 mph (61 knots) along a path from 2 miles west of Wauwatosa to just east of Cudahy. A downed power line sparked a house fire 3 miles southwest of downtown Milwaukee.

In addition to the tornado, a pole barn and other structures were damaged near Whitehall (Trempealeau County) and structural damage to buildings also occurred near Richland Center (Richland County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down near County Road F south of Arcadia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated thunderstorms, developing on the nose of a strengthening 850 mb wind maximum, produced hail up to 1 inch in diameter over portions of southern Wisconsin. The warm air advection regime pushed a warm front into the area with instability, reflected in enhanced levels of elevated CAPE around 1000 J/kg, allowing deep convection with deep layer shear enabling sustained updrafts that produced the large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast developing snow storm quickly developed across western Iowa Monday afternoon, April 22nd, and moved across northern Iowa, and into central Wisconsin by early Tuesday morning.  A mixture of rain and snow occurred over south central and east central Minnesota, as well as west central Wisconsin during the afternoon, but it quickly changed over to all snow by the early evening and became heavy at times. The mixture of rain and snow held on through 11 pm around Eau Claire, Menomonie and Chippewa Falls which kept totals under 1-3 inches. The heaviest snow fell along a narrow band from Spring Valley, northeast to Clear Lake, Rice Lake and Haugen.  These areas received between 5 to 7 inches, with locally 10 inches near Comstock and Almena in western Barron County. The snow tapered to flurries after 2 am and then ended around 5 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 8 inches across the county, with isolated 10 inch amounts near Comstock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unusual snow event unfolded across portions of south central and east central Minnesota, as well as west central Wisconsin on the evening of Wednesday, May 1st. Snowfall amounts topped over a foot of snow, with locally 12 to 15 inches in a small band near River Falls, Baldwin, Rice Lake and Ladysmith, Wisconsin, by the time the snow tapered off early Thursday afternoon, May 2nd. ||This system was highly unusual due to the fact that May snowfall amounts average less than an inch. A strong Canadian air mass for early May allowed for critical temperatures to lower enough to produce snow across south central and east central Minnesota during the late afternoon and early evening of Wednesday May 1st. Areas around Chippewa Falls, and Eau Clairie, Wisconsin, were warmer initially, which kept this area in a mixture of sleet and rain, before changing over to all snow by Thursday morning, May 2nd. ||A strong surge of moisture from the gulf interacted with the cold air mass to produce very heavy snowfall from Wednesday evening, through Thursday morning, before tapering off after 12 pm. Snowfall rates were as high as 1-2 inches per hour during the height of the storm. The following are some of the higher snowfall totals, ending Thursday afternoon, May 2nd. ||Near Ladysmith 15.0 inches|Rice Lake 14.0 inches. |Weyerhaeuser 14.0 inches. |River Falls 13.0 inches. |Baldwin 12.0 inches. |Jim Falls 11.4 inches. |Durand 10.5 inches. ||Both Ladysmith, and River Falls, broke the 24 hour, calendar day state record of 12.0 inches for the most snowfall in May.  Both these sites beat the previous Wisconsin state daily record in May of 10.9 inches.   ||Due to the heavy snowfall and high liquid water content, large tree branches broke off and caused damage to power lines, along with numerous power outages. ||Highland Ridge Campground at the Eau Galle Dam and Recreation Site, located in Spring Valley, Wisconsin, had excessive storm damage due to the extremely wet, and heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 10 inches across eastern Polk County with locally heavier amounts near Clear Lake, Wisconsin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, but quickly moving area of precipitation in the form of freezing rain, sleet and snow, quickly moved from southeastern Minnesota, northeast across west central Wisconsin by noon. Initially, a mixture of sleet and freezing rain occurred, but some of the precipitation turned over to light snow and sleet as it moved northward into |west central Wisconsin.  There was a period in the afternoon where snowfall rates were one inch per hour, but most of the heavier snowfall ended by early Sunday evening. Some lingering light snow and freezing drizzle occurred in portions of west central Wisconsin through midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observers reported two to four inches of snow, mainly in the far northern part of the county, by the time the storm tapered off early Sunday evening, but a mixture of sleet and freezing rain did occur during the onset early Sunday afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: No changes occurred during December, 2013 to the severe drought across western Wisconsin. Because the ground was frozen, no moisture from the 10 to 20 inches of snow that fell was able to work into the soil. As a result, the severe drought continued across the northern portions of Buffalo and Trempealeau Counties into the northwest corner of Jackson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No changes occurred to the severe drought across northern Buffalo County. Because the ground was frozen, no moisture from the 10 to 15 inches of snow that fell was able to soak into the soil.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: No changes occurred to the severe drought across the northwest corner of Jackson County. Because the ground was frozen, no moisture from the 15 to 20 inches of snow that fell was able to soak into the soil.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Upper Midwest had a long duration winter storm event that developed Monday morning, December 2nd, and eventually evolved into a large storm system that moved from the Plains, northeast across the Upper Midwest and western Great Lakes region by late Wednesday night. ||A weak disturbance produced locally two to five inches across Polk, Rusk and Barron Counties of west central Wisconsin from Monday morning to Tuesday morning. A few locations in Barron County near Rice Lake and Cumberland received locally six inches from Monday's storm. ||By Tuesday, much warmer air from the southern United States produced precipitation in the form of sleet, rain and snow that fell through part of Wednesday afternoon as the surface low moved across west central Wisconsin. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations up to two tenths of an inch from freezing rain were reported across western Wisconsin from a winter storm that impacted the region on January 27th. The winter storm originated over South Dakota and then moved almost due east into Wisconsin. As it did, it pulled a warm front north through the region. The warm air that came north with the warm front was responsible for the freezing rain. The warm air moved as far north as about Interstate 90, which is where sleet began mixing in with the freezing rain. Farther to the north, all the precipitation fell as snow. The freezing rain and sleet produced a coating of ice on most exposed surfaces leading to very slick roads with several accidents occurring. The highest reported ice accumulation was two tenths of an inch in Prairie du Chien (Crawford County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and sleet created a coating of ice on exposed surfaces leading to very slick roads and numerous accidents occurred across the county. The highest reported ice accumulation was two tenths of an inch in Prairie du Chien.

A few locations in Barron County near Rice Lake and Cumberland received locally six inches from Monday's storm. ||By Tuesday, much warmer air from the southern United States produced precipitation in the form of sleet, rain and snow that fell through part of Wednesday afternoon as the surface low moved across west central Wisconsin. The mixture of precipitation eventually changed over to all snow Wednesday evening. However, the mixture of sleet and snow added up to locally 4 to 7 inches across portions of west central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals by Wednesday evening ranged from 4 to 7 inches snow and sleet across the county.

Several inches of snow, along with a period of freezing rain, caused hazardous driving conditions across much of north central and northeast Wisconsin.  The heaviest snow fell in far north central Wisconsin where 4 to 6 inches of snow was followed by freezing rain and sleet.  The highest snowfall totals in Vilas County included an official report of 6.9 inches at Minocqua, and reports from the general public of 6.8 inches near Lac du Flambeau, 6.5 inches near Boulder Junction, and 6.0 inches at Arbor Vitae.||The precipitation formed as an area of low pressure moved across Wisconsin.  The same system dropped more than 2 feet of snow on northeast Minnesota.||Slick roads from a quick blast of snow and ice resulted in a multitude of traffic accidents in central Wisconsin.  One person was killed (indirect) and at least 2 people were injured (indirect) in the crashes.  Three roads were closed in Wausau (Marathon Co.) due to accidents and hazardous driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heaviest snowfall was in Vilas County, where 4 to 6 inches of snow fell, followed by freezing rain.

Snowfall amounts of 5 to 12 inches covered portions of western Wisconsin from a winter storm that moved through the region on January 30th. The storm originated over the southern Plains states and moved northeast through the Mississippi River Valley producing a blanket of heavy snow from central and eastern Iowa into Wisconsin. In addition to the snow, the storm produced northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph that created blowing and drifting snow that at times, lowered the visibility to a quarter mile. The timing of the storm, had a direct impact on the morning commute of the 30th. The highest reported snowfall amount was 11.5 inches in Richland Center (Richland County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow created hazardous conditions across the county, especially during the morning commute time. The visibility at times dropped as low a quarter of a mile in the snow and blowing snow. The highest reported snowfall total was 7.8 inches in Stueben.

Heavy snow on the northwest side of this system fell over parts of southwest into central Wisconsin. A band of 6 to 8 inches fell across Grant and Richland Counties into the southern sections of Adams and Juneau Counties. The highest reported total was 7.8 inches in Fennimore (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 5 to 8 inches of snow fell across Grant County. All but the far southeast corner of the county received at least 6 inches. The highest reported total was 7.8 inches in Fennimore.

A large, but quickly moving area of precipitation in the form of freezing rain, sleet and snow, quickly moved from southeastern Minnesota, northeast across west central Wisconsin by noon. Initially, a mixture of sleet and freezing rain occurred, but some of the precipitation turned over to light snow and sleet as it moved northward into |west central Wisconsin.  There was a period in the afternoon where snowfall rates were one inch per hour, but most of the heavier snowfall ended by early Sunday evening. Some lingering light snow and freezing drizzle occurred in portions of west central Wisconsin through midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observers reported two to four inches of snow, mainly in the far northern part of the county, by the time the storm tapered off early Sunday evening, but a mixture of sleet and freezing rain did occur during the onset early Sunday afternoon.  ||Dunn County authorities say slippery roads in western Wisconsin were suspected in a three-vehicle crash that killed one person and put another in the hospital.||The crash happened around 1 p.m. Sunday on State Highway 64 near Boyceville, north of Menomonie.

A school bus was struck near the rear wheels when the driver of the second vehicle did not see that the bus was turning. The automated weather observing equipment at the Viroqua airport reported dense fog with a visibility of a mile or less from around midnight January 11th into the early evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms, known as a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS), produced six tornadoes and widespread damaging winds across east-central Wisconsin during the late night hours of August 6-7.  The storms formed ahead of a cold front that also produced severe weather in Minnesota and western Wisconsin.||The tornadoes occurred within about 50 minutes--between 12:22 am and 1:10 am CDT.  Hundreds of homes, businesses and farm buildings were damaged. Two injuries occurred at a campground in Waupaca County.  Thousands of trees and power lines were also downed, leaving tens of thousands of people without power. The strongest tornado of the event reached EF2 intensity (winds estimated at 115 to 120 mph). This tornado caused significant damage to a church in New London. ||The six tornadoes that occurred on August 7 were the most nighttime tornadoes in a single event in northeast Wisconsin since accurate tornado records began (1950).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado that touched down in Outagamie County, 3.5 miles east of Greenville at 12:38 am CDT, briefly moved across far southwest Brown County near Holland. A few trees were knocked over by the tornado in Brown County (DI 28, DOD 3). The average path width was 150 yards. The tornado then moved into Calumet County at 12:50 am CDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed near Highway 41 northwest of Appleton and moved east across northern portions of Appleton, Kimberly and Kaukauna. The tornado caused some roof damage to the Northland Mall (DI 11, DOD 2) and caused roof and window damage to two small retail buildings (DI 8, DOD 3 and 4). The storm then knocked over four wood electrical poles (DI 24, DOD 2 and 3) and uprooted or heavily damaged hundreds of trees in a nearby neighborhood (DI 27, DOD 2 and 3; DI 28, DOD 2 and 3). Although the houses in the area sustained relatively minor wind damage directly, fallen trees caused considerable collateral damage to roofs, siding and vehicles. The tornado exited Outagamie County about four miles east-southeast of Kaukauna at 12:49 am CDT and continued east into far southwest Brown County, then into northern Calumet County. The average path width in Outagamie County was 125 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that developed across western Wisconsin in early September, shrunk in size during the first week in October. Between 1.5 and 5 inches of rain fell across the region, which allowed the drought to be downgraded to moderate across all of Adams, La Crosse and Vernon Counties. During the second week of October, another half to one inch of rain fell, which allowed the drought to be downgraded even more.  The severe drought continued in parts of Buffalo, Jackson, Juneau, Monroe and Trempealeau Counties, but was smaller in areal coverage. Non-irrigated corn fields across Adams, Juneau and Monroe Counties had plants that ranged from 2 to 5 feet tall and had 0 to 3 ears on each plant. Some soybean fields in the same counties had plants half their normal height.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought area across Monroe County shrunk in size after 1 to 3 inches of rain fell during the first week of October. Another half to one inch of rain fell during the second week of October which confined the severe drought area to the eastern three-quarters of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across portions of western Wisconsin after a very dry summer. Precipitation deficits of 6 to 8 inches were common from July 1st through the end of September. This, combined with above normal temperatures over the last half of August into September, caused the soil moisture to rapidly diminish leading to the development of the severe drought in portions of Buffalo, Clark, Jackson and Trempealeau Counties. Clark County has been one of the hardest hit counties by the drought. Corn was being chopped for silage since it never pollinated and thus did not develop any ears. Many farmers also did not get a third cutting of hay. Some trees across all of western Wisconsin became stressed because of the drought and dropped their leaves. Thus the display of fall colors had the potential to not be as brilliant as other years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across the northwest corner of Jackson County early in September and spread across the entire county by the end of the month. Precipitation deficits of 6 to 8 inches since the beginning of July, combined with above normal temperatures over the last half of August into September, led to the development of the drought.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranged in size from three-quarters of an inch to one inch in Owen.

Hundreds of homes, businesses and farm buildings were damaged. Two injuries occurred at a campground in Waupaca County. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado that formed in Outagamie County 3.5 miles east of Greenville at 12:38 am CDT, and briefly moved across Brown County, entered Calumet County 1.6 miles north of Forest Junction at 12:50 am CDT. The tornado damaged about three dozen trees (DI 28, DOD 3 and 4). Two metal truss towers collapsed under the weight of several high voltage power lines (that weighed two pounds per foot) which were likely moving in unison as their resonance frequency was reached. Engineers that inspected the damage estimated winds of 100 mph. Nearby trees sustained relatively minor damage (DI 28, DOD 2 and 3). The average path width in Calumet County was 125 yards.

Some trees across all of western Wisconsin became stressed because of the drought and dropped their leaves. Thus the display of fall colors had the potential to not be as brilliant as other years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across all but the southwest corner of Vernon County in mid September. Precipitation deficits of 6 to 8 inches since the beginning of July, combined with above normal temperatures over the last half of August into September, led to the development of the drought. By the end of the month, 1 to 3 inches of rain had fallen, allowing the drought to be upgraded to moderate across the southern quarter of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms, known as a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS), produced six tornadoes and widespread damaging winds across east-central Wisconsin during the late night hours of August 6-7.  The storms formed ahead of a cold front that also produced severe weather in Minnesota and western Wisconsin.||The tornadoes occurred within about 50 minutes--between 12:22 am and 1:10 am CDT.  Hundreds of homes, businesses and farm buildings were damaged. Two injuries occurred at a campground in Waupaca County.  Thousands of trees and power lines were also downed, leaving tens of thousands of people without power. The strongest tornado of the event reached EF2 intensity (winds estimated at 115 to 120 mph). This tornado caused significant damage to a church in New London. ||The six tornadoes that occurred on August 7 were the most nighttime tornadoes in a single event in northeast Wisconsin since accurate tornado records began (1950).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 mph was recorded by the automated weather observing system at the Outagamie County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms developed along an occluded front lifting through southern Wisconsin during the afternoon and early evening hours of October 5th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many cars disabled due to severe street flooding in the city of Waukesha. Street flooding throughout eastern Waukesha County. Several manhole covers removed from flooding.  The new Health and Human Services Building in the city of Waukesha was flooded.

This tornado caused significant damage to a church in New London. ||The six tornadoes that occurred on August 7 were the most nighttime tornadoes in a single event in northeast Wisconsin since accurate tornado records began (1950).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado that initially formed in Outagamie County 2.7 miles east-northeast of Mackville at 12:40 am CDT, and moved across Brown County and the Fox Valley, entered Manitowoc County about 4.1 miles northwest of Rosecrans at 1:01 am CDT. The tornado damaged several outbuildings (DI 1, DOD 3; DI 1, DOD 4) and damaged the roof of a barn (DI 1, DOD 2). A 110 year old stone structure at the Maribel Caves County Park was knocked over by the strong winds. Hundreds of trees were damaged or uprooted along the path of the tornado (DI 27, DOD 2 and 3; DI 28, DOD 3 and 4). The damage survey revealed the tornado ended a couple miles east of Maribel, but straight-line wind damage continued into the eastern part of the county. The average path width of the tornado in Manitowoc County was 125 yards.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado that developed 2.7 miles east-northeast of Mackville in Outagamie County at 12:40 am CDT moved into Brown County, entering near Highway 41, 1.8 miles northwest of Wrightstown, at 12:48 am CDT. The tornado first hit the Apple Creek Campground, snapping 50 trees and knocking over two camping trailers. As the tornado moved across the southern part of the county, at least 70 homes sustained at least minor damage, many from fallen trees (DI 27, DOD 3 and 4; DI 28, DOD 3 and 4). The tornado entered Manitowoc County 4.1 miles northwest of Rosecrans at 1:01 am CDT. Average path width of the tornado in Brown County was 175 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across portions of western Wisconsin after a very dry summer. Precipitation deficits of 6 to 8 inches were common from July 1st through the end of September. This, combined with above normal temperatures over the last half of August into September, caused the soil moisture to rapidly diminish leading to the development of the severe drought in portions of Buffalo, Clark, Jackson and Trempealeau Counties. Clark County has been one of the hardest hit counties by the drought. Corn was being chopped for silage since it never pollinated and thus did not develop any ears. Many farmers also did not get a third cutting of hay. Some trees across all of western Wisconsin became stressed because of the drought and dropped their leaves. Thus the display of fall colors had the potential to not be as brilliant as other years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across the northern half of Buffalo County early in September and spread across the entire county by the end of the month. Precipitation deficits of 6 to 8 inches since the beginning of July, combined with above normal temperatures over the last half of August into September, led to the development of the drought.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought (D2) conditions that developed across far southwest Wood County in September 2013 came to an end in early October 2013.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the first week of November, 1 to 2 inches of rain fell across western into central Wisconsin. This helped the lingering drought, lowering the drought status to moderate across all of Juneau and Monroe Counties. However, this was not enough rain to have a significant impact in other parts of western Wisconsin and the severe drought continued across the northern halves of Buffalo and Trempealeau Counties into the northwest corner of Jackson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought status across Juneau County was changed from severe to moderate after 1 to 2 inches of rain fell in the first week of November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed during the early afternoon across southeast Minnesota and then intensified as they moved across portions of western Wisconsin. At their strongest, the storms produced large spiky hailstones with some estimated to have almost 3 inch diameters. Despite the large size, very little damage occurred except in Onalaska (La Crosse County) where two car dealerships were hit by the large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail fell on the northeast side of Onalaska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening of August 1st across parts of southwest Wisconsin. Some of these storms became strong enough to produce large hail across Richland County. The largest hail reported was quarter sized in and near Excelsior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging in size from dimes to quarters fell in Excelsior.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that developed over northern Minnesota along a cold front, advanced into Wisconsin during the evening of August 6th. The storms became oriented into a solid line as they moved across parts of western and central Wisconsin and produced large hail and damaging winds. The largest hail reported was half dollar sized in Nelson (Buffalo County). The winds from the storms blew the roof off a pole barn near Withee (Clark County) and lifted the roof off a county building, damaged crops and broke windows just outside of Sparta (Monroe County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in Eleva.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of severe storms developed in an unstable air mass ahead of an advancing cold front. The storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts, at times in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph was recorded at Austin Straubel Airport on the southwest side of Green Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of severe storms developed in an unstable air mass ahead of an advancing cold front. The storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts, at times in excess of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest flow of hot and humid air resulted in three days with high temperatures in the 90s and heat indices around 100 across east-central Wisconsin. A middle-aged female died as a direct result of the heat at her residence in the city of Manitowoc.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A middle-aged female died at her residence in the city of Manitowoc after a nearly three-day heatwave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a broken line of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of July 22nd.  A small tornado touched down in the Mississippi River and moved onshore into the Trempealeau National Wildlife Refuge (Trempealeau County) before dissipating. The only damage from the tornado was to hundreds of trees in the refuge. Otherwise, the storms produced a few more funnel clouds and some large hail. The largest hail reported was golf ball sized in Centerville (Trempealeau County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted over the Mississippi River southeast of Fountain City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that developed in South Dakota and moved across Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin, produced wind damage in Star Prairie Township less than a mile northeast of the Village of Somerset.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of severe storms developed in an unstable air mass ahead of an advancing cold front. The storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts, at times in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm with estimated winds of 85 to 95 mph, caused damage to trees and two houses in Gresham. Both houses lost portions of the roof deck, in addition to several dozen trees on the property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of severe storms developed in an unstable air mass ahead of an advancing cold front. The storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts, at times in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees in Keshena. One tree fell onto a car and killed a 39 year old woman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of severe storms developed in an unstable air mass ahead of an advancing cold front. The storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts, at times in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported trees down on 14th Street in Clintonville.  Time of event was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front over northern Wisconsin during the afternoon of August 21st. Some of these storms caused minor tree damage in Reseburg Township (Clark County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches were blown down in Reseburg Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed during the early afternoon across southeast Minnesota and then intensified as they moved across portions of western Wisconsin. At their strongest, the storms produced large spiky hailstones with some estimated to have almost 3 inch diameters. Despite the large size, very little damage occurred except in Onalaska (La Crosse County) where two car dealerships were hit by the large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail with a diameter of 2.5 inches fell just north of Interstate 90 on French Island.
