At their strongest, the storms produced large spiky hailstones with some estimated to have almost 3 inch diameters. Despite the large size, very little damage occurred except in Onalaska (La Crosse County) where two car dealerships were hit by the large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail fell in Onalaska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a broken line of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of July 22nd.  A small tornado touched down in the Mississippi River and moved onshore into the Trempealeau National Wildlife Refuge (Trempealeau County) before dissipating. The only damage from the tornado was to hundreds of trees in the refuge. Otherwise, the storms produced a few more funnel clouds and some large hail. The largest hail reported was golf ball sized in Centerville (Trempealeau County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree branches were blown down northeast of Stoddard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm day combined with drought conditions led to the development of Dust Devils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dust devil moved from land to the Lake Monona shoreline and tipped over a Pontoon boat and the hoist that it was sitting on. Pier damage also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of severe storms developed in an unstable air mass ahead of an advancing cold front. The storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts, at times in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds, estimated at 80 mph, took down several trees, numerous large limbs and blew off pieces of siding and shingles of several houses on the west side of Green Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough combined with a warm, unstable air mass and led to the development of a series of thunderstorms across northeast Wisconsin.  Trees were knocked down by strong thunderstorm winds in several locations.  Rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 4 inches over parts of northern Wisconsin which caused localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down large tree limbs along Highway 52 and County Highway Z.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed during the early afternoon across southeast Minnesota and then intensified as they moved across portions of western Wisconsin. At their strongest, the storms produced large spiky hailstones with some estimated to have almost 3 inch diameters. Despite the large size, very little damage occurred except in Onalaska (La Crosse County) where two car dealerships were hit by the large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree trunk was split by thunderstorm winds with part of the tree falling on a vehicle parked under it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Eleven people were injured in an early morning accident north of Plainville (Adams County). A van carrying farm workers was rear ended by a semi-truck when the van slowed to turn off of State Highway 82. Due to visibilities near zero in dense fog at the time, the driver of the truck never saw the van turning off the road. None of the injuries were life threatening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eleven farm workers were injured when the van they were riding in was rear ended by a semi-truck. The accident occurred in dense fog, with the visibility near zero, when the van slowed to turn off State Highway 82 and the driver of the semi-truck did not see that the van was slowing down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced flooding rains over portions of South Central Wisconsin and far southern Southeast Wisconsin. The storms produced damaging wind gusts over Lafayette and Dane counties. The storms formed on moderate low-level flow ahead of short wave trough that interacted with an existing outflow boundary over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported streets flooded with water up to 3 feet deep. Roads were closed with water rising halfway up on vehicles. Mudslides closed Badger Road at Circle Drive. Fallen trees closed another road in south central Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved from east central Wisconsin to eastern Upper Michigan. South winds brought unseasonably unstable air into southeast Wisconsin ahead of an approaching cold front. Multiple lines of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across southeast Wisconsin.

Low pressure intensified as it moved from east central Wisconsin to eastern Upper Michigan. South winds brought unseasonably unstable air into southeast Wisconsin ahead of an approaching cold front. Multiple lines of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across southeast Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets flooded from heavy rain. Northbound lanes of intersection of Layton and Howell Avenue is flooded and closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a broken line of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of July 22nd.  A small tornado touched down in the Mississippi River and moved onshore into the Trempealeau National Wildlife Refuge (Trempealeau County) before dissipating. The only damage from the tornado was to hundreds of trees in the refuge. Otherwise, the storms produced a few more funnel clouds and some large hail. The largest hail reported was golf ball sized in Centerville (Trempealeau County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began as a waterspout in the marsh waters just off the main channel of the Mississippi River southeast of Winona, Minnesota, but on the Wisconsin side.  White spray could be seen for some distance as the waterspout pulled up water into the funnel.  The tornado then moved onshore in the Trempealeau Wildlife Refuge and knocked down numerous trees for nearly a half mile.  Water plants were found on the refuge roads suggesting they were thrown some distance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms rolled across western Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of May 19th. The first round of storms produced wind damage across Buffalo County during the afternoon. The second round of storms in the evening produced additional severe wind gusts in Vernon, Grant and Richland Counties. The most significant damage occurred in Gilmanton (Buffalo County) were a dozen power poles were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across U.S. Highway 14/61 in Westby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in far northeast South Dakota, and moved east-southeast across Minnesota, and eventually into west-central Wisconsin during the late afternoon and evening hours of Tuesday, August 6th. One storm produced half dollar size hail near Rice Lake, while other severe storms came from a complex of severe storms across east-central Minnesota during the evening hours, and produced some damaging wind gusts and quarter size hail.

A couple clusters of thunderstorms moved from southwest to northeast across southern Wisconsin during the late afternoon and evening hours as a cold front approached from Minnesota and Iowa. Severe thunderstorm wind damage occurred with only one severe hail report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches 4 inch diameter snapped and downed power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms crossed southern Wisconsin during the afternoon hours of July 21st. The storms produced damaging wind gusts as well as hail up to 1 inch. Two people were hurt when their horses were struck by lightning while riding in the Kettle Moraine Forest near Eagle in Waukesha County. The thunderstorms formed in a region of focused warm air advection, with lift aided by vorticity advection from a short wave that tracked across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dane County 911 call Center reported a roof was blown off a home 1 mile south of De Forest with debris from the roof damaging other homes by a possible gustnado as two outflow boundaries collided over the area. Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph (52 knots).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of severe thunderstorms developed along a slowly southeast moving cold front.

Several lines of severe thunderstorms crossed southern Wisconsin during the evening hours of May 14th. The storms produced damaging wind gusts up to 70 to 75 mph (60 to 65 knots). Widespread damage was reported in swaths up to 6 miles in width, which included structural damage to homes and farm buildings as well as downed trees and power lines due to the powerful winds. WE Energies reported 23,000 customers in Dodge, Jefferson, Waukesha and Milwaukee counties were without power at the height of the storm. The severe thunderstorms formed along a warm front that extended from weak low pressure in northwest Wisconsin south of Superior to near Chicago. While wind shear and instability were marginal, evaporative cooling as the rain fell into dry air in the lower levels produced prime conditions for downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported numerous trees and power lines downed by thunderstorm wind gusts, measured by a trained spotter at 68 mph (59 knots). The damage was reported along an 8-mile wide swath from 1.5 miles west of Oconomowoc to 1 mile east of Elm Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms developed along an occluded front lifting through southern Wisconsin during the afternoon and early evening hours of October 5th. Very heavy rain and flash flooding occurred over southeast Wisconsin. Over south central Wisconsin, a thunderstorm produced an EF0 tornado in Marquette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado began near the intersection of County Highway P and State Highway 23 just west of the I-39 interchange about 1.2 miles southwest of Endeavor. The tornado moved north along a 1.78 mile long path ending just under 1 mile northwest of Endeavor about 1/2 mile north of Gail Avenue. The maximum path width was 50 yards. Trees were snapped along the path, with one tree falling on a house and car, destroying the car and damaging the roof of the house. Other homes had shingle damage with a section of metal roof peeled off of a farm house. The wind damage was consistent with an EF-0 tornado with winds of 70 to 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance passed across the area during peak daytime heating.  The unstable air, the upper disturbance, and a dew point boundary contributed to the development of thunderstorms that produced large hail and a funnel cloud in east central Wisconsin.  Quite a bit of quarter to walnut size hail fell in and near Appleton, and there was even some golf ball to nearly tennis ball size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced lime size hail as it passed near Stephensville.

The unstable air, the upper disturbance, and a dew point boundary contributed to the development of thunderstorms that produced large hail and a funnel cloud in east central Wisconsin.  Quite a bit of quarter to walnut size hail fell in and near Appleton, and there was even some golf ball to nearly tennis ball size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced walnut size hail at Appleton Medical Central and near the intersection of Wisconsin Avenue and Mason Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across South Central Wisconsin with a cold front during the evening hours of July 22nd, producing large hail, damaging wind gusts and heavy downpours of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported trees blown down by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated up to 60 mph (58 knots) in Brodhead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable air mass combined with an upper level disturbance and a weak surface boundary to produce thunderstorms.  Some of the storms produced large, destructive hail and isolated wind damage.  Quarter to golf ball size hail damaged vehicles in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), but east central Wisconsin was hardest hit by the storms.  Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines in the Birnamwood (Shawano Co.) and Clintonville (Waupaca Co.) areas, and golf ball to tennis ball size hail caused considerable damage to vehicles, homes and businesses around the city of Manitowoc (Manitowoc Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced quarter size hail west of Rhinelander.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first severe thunderstorms developed during the evening hours of April 9th. These storms were north of a warm front that was draped across central Iowa. The storms produced large hail across southern La Crosse County with up to quarter sized hail falling on the south side of the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County). A car dealership on the south side of La Crosse was hit by the hail with an estimated 170 cars receiving minor dents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After several rounds of rainfall, the Kickapoo River at Readstown went above flood stage two times between April 10th and April 12th. The first round of flooding occurred on the 10th with a crest of 11.06 feet. The second round of flooding occurred on the 11th and 12th with a crest of 11.34 feet on the 12th. Flood stage at Readstown is 11 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of severe storms developed in an unstable air mass ahead of an advancing cold front. The storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts, at times in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 1.00 inch diameter hail at Lost Lake or approximately 4 miles East-Southeast of the town of Tipler.
